responseSet.title: Ah, The French
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ah, The French

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Steven Levy  

W. C. Fields, in his terse commentary on French psychoanalysis and pluralism, captures in his dyspepsia the attitude of most North American psychoanalysts toward Gallic contributions to our collective wisdom. To this attitude should be added, for balance, a long history of anti-Americanism among the French that certainly antedates the birth of psychoanalysis. It is into this history that the trouble I am alluding to is to be fitted. In this issue, Mitchell Wilson contributes a piece sure to stir up the controversies that swirl around pluralism and the misunderstandings and anxieties it creates. While psychoanalytic pluralism began with Freud and Breuer and their differences, which were diplomatically contained in their joint writings, our present era is characterized by an ever widening panoply of theories, schools, and techniques that we increasingly accommodate under the psychoanalytic umbrella. Where alternative schools historically were extruded from the mainstream, today there is no mainstream. Wilson writes about a traditionally “French” subject that he attempts to formulate to American sensibilities. Our commentators take up one or another of the issues such translation requires.

This entire issue of JAPA is concerned with our pluralistic psychoanalytic world, at its core conceding grudgingly that the study of personal meaning allows for—some would say demands—different readings that lean on personal, organizational, national, and cultural contexts. Whether we are thereby enriched or hamstrung is a question every psychoanalyst faces. My own experience, which likely mirrors that of many in my generation, has been to embrace a hard-won pragmatic ecumenicism. It is now more important for me to understand and learn from a diverse group of colleagues than to feel I have cornered the truth. From another perspective, my need for friends has outstripped my propensity for building impenetrable walls as the world has grown increasingly challenging of psychoanalytic ideas.

responseSet.title: Research-Informed Reflections on the Processes of Introjection and Identification: Commentary on Olds
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Research-Informed Reflections on the Processes of Introjection and Identification: Commentary on Olds

Jesse Geller

Jesse Geller

389 Whitney Avenue
New Haven, CT 06511
E-mail: jessegeller23@aol.com

Jesse Geller   Associate Clinical Professor of Psychology, Department of Psychiatry, Yale University.

David Olds's essay gives us a great deal to think about. Multiple readings are required to assimilate his synthesis of the rapidly accumulating information generated from studies of the basic mechanisms of the brain, the biological underpinnings of imitative learning, the genetic components of identification, and the varied forms of human memory. The primary aim of my commentary is to extend and reflect on the implications of his essay in terms of the complex interplay of the processes of internalization and representation as they manifest themselves in the context of psychotherapy and psychotherapy supervision.

Three questions will be addressed. Does the current intellectual climate support Olds's conviction that psychoanalysis will move in a convergent path with the neuro- and cognitive sciences? How shall we conceptualize the relation between introjective and identificatory modes of internalization? Can empirical research on the ways in which patients in psychotherapy internalize the therapeutic relationship enhance our understanding of mental disorders and sharpen our therapeutic skills?

Differentiation Vs. Integration

The literatures of all psychotherapies offer important teachings. But for the most part the intellectual traditions that guide the work of psychotherapists have developed in separate, noncommunicating worlds.

59

responseSet.title: Studying Psychoanalysis: Asking and Answering the Hard Questions
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Studying Psychoanalysis: Asking and Answering the Hard Questions

Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser

E-mail: shauser@jbcc.harvard.edu

Stuart Hauser  

How do we study psychoanalysis—its basic constructs, underlying theoretical frameworks, and clinical practice—in creative and productive ways? Running throughout Patrick Lutyen, Sidney Blatt, and Jozef Corveleyn's challenging target paper and the exchange with Donald Spence and Richard Almond is this deep and challenging question. Despite answers offered by proponents of particular positions, singular solutions to this complex problem are not yet readily available. Our target paper lucidly delineates several long-standing debates about psychoanalytic research, explicating the assumptions underlying both various perspectives and the polarizations that fuel these debates and on occasion are sharpened by them.

“Minding the Gap” refers to the seemingly vast differences between investigators' data-analytic and interpretive practices. Luyten and his coauthors delineate two “camps” or “schools of thought” within psychoanalysis; each is linked with different methods for studying clinical and theoretical questions, “one more clinical in orientation, more focused on meaning and interpretation, and relying primarily on the traditional case study method, the other more research-oriented, focused on cause-and-effect relationships, and relying primarily on methods borrowed from the natural and social sciences.” The case study “school” is most often associated with methods drawn from traditions within the humanities—literature, history, cultural studies—that most prominently employ narrative and hermeneutic approaches. The possibility for synergy—intermethodological collaborations—between such different approaches is at times raised by the authors. Yet whether these methods are ripe for such collaborative linkage remains problematic, a significant point raised by Spence: “it may be too early to choose between the two schools of investigation outlined in the target paper— the two opposing camps facing each other across the gap”.

561

[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Theory

The Quiet Revolution in American Psychoanalysis: Selected Papers of Arnold M. Cooper. Edited by Elizabeth Auchincloss. Hove and New York: Brunner-Routledge, 2005, 287 pp., $33.95.

Harry Trosman

Harry Trosman

5841 South Maryland Avenue
Chicago, IL 60637-1470
Fax: 773-702-6454
E-mail: Htrosman@yoda.bsd.uchicago.edu

Harry Trosman   Training and Supervising Analyst, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis; Professor of Psychiatry, University of Chicago.

The weapons of controversy require little application when confronted by the selected papers of Arnold M. Cooper. This collection, edited by Elizabeth L. Auchincloss, who provides an excellent introduction, is one of a series of publications of the New Library of Psychoanalysis, a new version of the International Psychoanalytical Library associated with the Institute of Psychoanalysis, London. This series of more than fifty publications presents the writings of many of the most prominent British and Continental psychoanalysts. The Quiet Revolution in American Psychoanalysis is unique in that it speaks from an American point of view

The work of Cooper, once considered to be that of a maverick, would today be considered by many, including me, to be solidly in the mainstream. Trained at the Columbia institute and markedly influenced by the culturalist and adaptational point of view, Cooper today speaks with a voice that American psychoanalysts find quite reasonable. His thought is deeply wedded to praxis; theoretical preoccupations unrelated to technique he finds trivial, as he considers analysis to be fundamentally a clinical transaction. His American pragmatism brings with it a freedom from doctrine, from metapsychology for its own sake, and an unwillingness to subscribe to authority in the face of the clinical fact. Cooper favors an open and generally pluralist position, although he would be the first to acknowledge that some ideas are better than others.

The clinical immersion he emphasizes leads to a position with which it is easy to resonate. Cooper's work questions the centrality of the “classically” defined oedipus complex as the nuclear dynamic in both personality development and therapeutic work.

657

The Unsung Psychoanalyst: The Quiet Influence of Ruth Easser. By Mary Kay O'Neil. Toronto: University of Toronto Press, 2004. pp. 240. pp. $45.00..

Joan Lentz

Joan Lentz

821 Marquette Avenue
Suite 1410
Minneapolis, MN 55402
Fax: 612-339-5220
E-mail: joanlentz@aol.com

Joan Lentz   Faculty, Minnesota Psychoanalytic Institute.

Combining the personal story of psychoanalyst Ruth Easser, a description of the psychoanalytic milieu of her time, and an examination of life as a psychoanalyst today, Mary Kay O'Neil succeeds in weaving a complex and intriguing tale in The Unsung Psychoanalyst: The Quiet

698

Influence of Ruth Easser. Taking as its point of departure Easser's entry into the field of psychoanalysis in 1946, the book surveys the latter half of the first century of psychoanalysis. In a foreword, Arnold Cooper sketches an historical and political overview of that period. For psychoanalysis, it was at once a time of prestige and rigidity. Cooper outlines the emergence of dissenting voices, of pioneering institutions, of attempts to expand the confining bounds of what was considered acceptable theory and technique within the field, and he reviews the forces from without that have stimulated change. At its heart, the book is about Easser, a relatively unknown but gifted clinician and teacher who died in 1975 at the age of fifty-three. Along with the usual source material, O'Neil makes use of her personal knowledge of her subject, having been in a training analysis with Easser the last two years of her life. In addition, O'Neil allows the people connected professionally with Easser to tell their own stories of becoming and being analysts. Including the recollections of the many psychoanalysts who were interviewed by O'Neil adds a rich texture to the tapestry that emerges of psychoanalysis then and now.

But O'Neil's main purpose in writing this book is to tell the story of all psychoanalysts through a recounting of Ruth Easser's life. Thus, she uses each aspect of Easser's professional life to explore a larger picture of life as a modern psychoanalyst. The book moves from past to present and back, tracing evolution and continuity, alternating between a narrative of Easser's life, which includes commentaries by her contemporaries, and a discussion of the issues of current practice? from the practical to the profound. O'Neil explains in the introduction that little is known about the lives of analysts who are primarily clinicians and teachers. They are known mainly within their local institutes. Dedicated to their analysands and students, they are immediate role models for the next generation. They are the unsung psychoanalysts.

[[RunningHead]]

Listening to Fear: Helping Kids from Nightmares to the Nightly News. By Steven Marans. New York: Henry Holt, 2005, 264 pp., $15.00.

Ruth Hall

Ruth Hall

1956 South Belvoir Boulevard
South Euclid, OH 44121
E-mail: rdek1@aol.com

Ruth Hall   Faculty, Hanna Perkins Center Child Therapy Training Program.

“Lord save the little children! Because for every child born of woman's womb there is a time of running through a shadowed place, an alley with no doors, and a hunter whose footsteps ring brightly along the

1441

bricks behind him. With every child-rich or poor-however warm and safe the nursery, there is a time of echoing and vast aloneness, when there is no one to come nor to hear, and dry leaves scurrying past along a street become the rustle of Dread and the ticking of the old house is the cocking of the hunter's gun” (Davis Grubb, Night of the Hunter).

This quote, the epigraph to Listening to Fear, eloquently captures the emotional state of terror that occurs when the ego's ability to know what is true is unstable and a sense of total helplessness prevails. If it is brief and vanishes with the comfort of the appearance of a trusted other, the aftermath, while notable, does not broadly compromise functioning. However, if it persists “too long,” it constitutes trauma and reparative work is needed. Steven Marans's book aims to help caretakers of all sorts assist in that work. His main tool is the art of truly hearing what the child has to say about the traumatic event and treating it with serious respect. He sifts it through his understanding of the power of fantasy and self-doubt and their roots in the vicissitudes of each developmental stage. He is especially mindful of the child's need to draw strength from primary caretakers, as soon as possible and throughout the recovery process. Marans asks the question, “How do we learn to listen?” His book is his attempt to answer that question.

He begins with the need for caretakers to allow themselves to remember their own times of fear as children as a way of “standing in the shoes” of the frightened child. He devotes two chapters to describing the adult experience of maintaining a daily sense of equilibrium. He identifies the subtle ways we all use of keeping mute the background noise of our stream of conscious associations to the impact of events. He provides a clear list of the things that threaten his equilibrium and the links to the fears of childhood.

[[RunningHead]]

Closing The Gap: Commentary on Luyten, Blatt, and Corveleyn

Donald Spence

Donald Spence

9 Haslet Avenue
Princeton, NJ 08540
E-mail: dpshaslet@aol.com

Donald Spence   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Robert Wood Johnson Medical School, Piscataway, NJ.

In the standard Darwinian picture of how ideas compete in a developing field, it is usually assumed that the open market tends to reward the best procedure and the most efficient technique. Creative solutions scramble in what has been called the adaptive landscape and the most fitting survive to reach, by slow degrees, the evolutionary high ground-the peak of natural selection. The system works largely because each advance must compete with what has gone before and because nature rewards the fittest with longer life and the widest audience-the build-a-better-mousetrap principle. But in the analytic landscape, survival of the fittest ideas and techniques is buffered by the fallibility of memory and the heavy hand of the case study tradition. Following Freud's example, we are all tempted to tell stories about our patients and to capture the gist of an hour with a vignette or anecdote. Such paraphrases may do partial justice to the original happening, but in no sense can they be called data; as we have repeatedly discovered, they reveal little if anything that could be intensively studied or reexamined. What is more, tradition tends to control what is remembered. Lacking a truly observational language, clinical accounts tend to fall under the influence of received theory. Even if a verbatim extract is presented (a rare occurrence), the reader is left without the controlling context and as a result can easily misunderstand what was meant. We are all familiar with Freud's specimen dream, but we are still waiting for a specimen interpretation.

Heavy dependence on memory and the hovering influence of the case study tradition have left us with a clinical past peopled by many stories but almost no data.

619

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis in Textbooks of Introductory Psychology: A Review

Sandra Park and Elizabeth Auchincloss

Elizabeth Auchincloss

113 ½ East 62nd Street
New York, NY 10021
E-mail: sanwpark@gmail.com

Elizabeth Auchincloss  

The fields of psychoanalysis and of academic psychology were born within a decade of one another at the end of the nineteenth century. The founder of the discipline of academic psychology is generally considered to be Wilhelm Wundt (1832-1902). After more than twenty years working to develop “physiological psychology” under the direction of Hermann von Helmholtz at the University of Berlin's Institute of Physiology, Wundt established the first institute of psychology at the University of Leipzig in 1879. The new institute was granted official status in 1883.

In the United States, the first university-based instruction in psychology was offered by the Harvard philosopher William James (himself a student of Helmholtz in the late 1860s), who began lecturing on the subject of German physiological psychology in 1875. The first university-based psychology laboratory in America was established at Johns Hopkins by G. Stanley Hall in 1883. The first professorship of psychology was established at the University of Pennsylvania in 1888. A year later, James himself was named Professor of Psychology (as opposed to Philosophy) at Harvard. Within two decades, at least two dozen American universities were offering courses in psychology, many taught by former students of Wundt. By 1902 three psychology journals were being published, and in that year the American Psychological Association was established. In 1909, when Freud visited Clark University at the invitation of G. Stanley Hall (by then president of that university and himself a former student of Wundt), he was not venturing into terrain where no psychologist had ever gone.

Many authors have examined the relationship between the disciplines of academic psychology and psychoanalysis in the United States. According to most, this relationship began on a reasonably friendly note.

Comings and Goings

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Steven Levy  

Choosing works to be published, helping authors express themselves effectively, and assembling manuscripts, commentaries, book reviews, and essays into cohesive and evocative journal issues is a creative and challenging enterprise. Our editorial board members, in joining the scholarly discourse of our field, are in a privileged position that I hope more than adequately compensates for the hundreds of hours they contribute during their three-year terms on our board. Working with the journal board has been among the major pleasures of my tenure as editor.

It is time to say good-bye and thank you to outgoing board members Stanley Coen, Daniel Jacobs, Anton Kris, and Donald Moss, and to editorial associates Sarah Ackerman, Ephi Betan, William Glover, and Jeffrey Stern. We will miss each of them and look forward to working with them again in the future.

Welcome to new board members Silvia Bell, Deborah Cabaniss, Melvin Lansky, Howard Levine, Jennifer Stuart, and Mitchell Wilson, and to new associates Daria Colombo, J. Timothy Davis, Zenobia Grusky, and Kevin Kelly. They bring rich interests and experience to JAPA. Readers can rest assured that what they read in our pages has passed through the hands and minds of gifted scholars, teachers, and clinicians dedicated to the best in psychoanalysis.

Misunderstanding Freud. By Arnold Goldberg. New York: Other Press, 2004, 260 pp., $35.00.

Steven Goldberg

Steven Goldberg

2116 Sutter Street
San Francisco, CA 94115
Fax: 415-346-5213
E-mail: stevenhgoldberg@sbcglobal.net

Steven Goldberg   Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute; Personal and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California

The core of Arnold Goldberg's new book is his concern with what he views as the two most prevalent “misunderstandings” in current psychoanalytic discourse. The first is the attempt to reduce it to neurobiology; the second is the attempt to reduce psychoanalysis to the study of social relationships. Discussion of these, and other, “misunderstandings” leads the author toward his own preferred understanding of both the nature and the boundaries of the psychoanalytic endeavor. This is subsumed under the rubric of psychoanalysis as the in-depth study of understanding and misunderstanding, especially as it pertains to unconscious mental life. The movement from understanding to misunderstanding to interpretation and finally to new understanding is the hermeneutic circle, the study of which is a second theme of this book.

[[RunningHead]]

A British Independent Analyst at Work

Jacqueline McGregor

Jacqueline McGregor

Houston Medical Center Professional Building
6655 Travis Street, Suite 700
Houston, TX 77030
E-mail: jcm@bcm.tmc.edu

Jacqueline McGregor   Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Boston, June 20, 2003.

Defining and characterizing the British Independent Group is a difficult undertaking, as the group itself is a heterogeneous and amorphous one that defies easy identification. The purpose of the panel discussion was to provide an example of one British Independent psychoanalyst's thinking about psychoanalytic work using a case presentation as a stimulus for discussion.

Paul Williams, chair of the panel and himself a British Independent, opened with a brief overview of the history of the British Psychoanalytical Society, which was created in 1919, mostly by analysts who had trained in Vienna and Berlin. In the 1930s a schism evolved between Melanie Klein and Anna Freud that led to the Controversial Discussions of the 1940s. Most of the British Society wanted to avoid taking sides; initially they were known as the “Middle Group” and later as the “Independents.” Although they do not espouse a particular theory, its members share a certain autonomous philosophy and personality. Williams cited two books as providing helpful background on the group: Eric Rayner's The Independent Mind in British Psychoanalysis and Gregorio Kohon's The British School of Psychoanalysis: The Independent Tradition.

Williams continued with highlights of the contributions the British Independents have made to psychoanalysis. Perhaps best known of these are infant development and the influence of the environment and trauma. The Independents conceptualized that the effects of early trauma on an individual were stored in memories, which were frozen or disassociated from a person's central ego or functional self. This was a further elaboration on the role of repression in the preoedipal origins of psychopathology.

Panelists: Paul Williams (chair), Jay Phillips, Nasir Ilahi.

[[RunningHead]]

Edoardo Weiss: The House that Freud Built. By Paul Roazen. New Brunswick, NJ: Transaction Publishers, 2005143 pp., $34.95..

Boyd Burris

Boyd Burris

Suite 310
4545 42nd Street NW
Washington, DC 20016-4623
Fax: 301-656-6263
E-mail: boydburris@cs.com

Boyd Burris   Training and Supervising Analyst, Baltimore-Washington Institute for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Georgetown University Medical Center and George Washington University Medical Center.

Paul Roazen, the well-known historian of psychoanalysis, in 1968 published Freud: Political and Social Thought, followed in 1969 by Brother Animal: The Story of Freud and Tausk. He subsequently published many books on the history of psychoanalysis, including biographies of Erik Erikson (1976) and Helene Deutsch (1985) and books that deal with the lives of analysts such as Sandor Rado and Edward Glover. Before any of these books were published, Roazen met with Edoardo Weiss (1889-1970), the recognized founder of Italian psychoanalysis who later practiced in Chicago, for twelve interviews in 1965 and 1966. The interviews form a considerable basis of the present book, which successfully combines an interesting and informative biographical study of Weiss with a mini-history of early psychoanalysis. The eminence of Sigmund Freud is always in the background of the book's story, and in choosing the subtitle The House That Freud Built Roazen acknowledged how much the story is about Freud as well as about Weiss.

The twelve interviews deal with, among other things, a correspondence between Weiss and Freud on clinical matters. At the time, Roazen had just started interviewing Helene Deutsch in Cambridge in pursuit of information from living members of Freud's original circle. He contacted Weiss in Chicago because he “knew there had been letters from Freud to Weiss” (p. 10). These letters (written between 1919 and 1936) would eventually be published by Weiss as Sigmund Freud as a Consultant: Recollections of a Pioneer in Psychoanalysis, but not until 1970, the year Weiss died.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychodynamic Therapy for Borderline Personality Disorder and Co-Occurring Alcohol use Disorders: A Newly Designed Ongoing Study

Robert Gregory , Susan Chlebowski , David Kang , Anna Remen and Maureen Soderberg

Maureen Soderberg

Syracuse, NY 13210
E-mail: gregoryr@upstate.edu

Maureen Soderberg  

Borderline personality disorder (BPD) is a serious illness that commonly co-occurs with substance misuse, especially alcohol use disorders (AUD). Approximately 50% of psychiatric inpatients with BPD also meet diagnostic criteria for AUD (Zanarini et al. 2004). Among patients being treated for AUD, the prevalence of BPD ranges from 16 to 22% (Morgenstern et al. 1997).

Co-occurring BPD worsens the outcome of alcohol recovery, including unplanned discharges and multiple measures of problem drinking (Morgenstern et al. 1997). Likewise, co-occurring AUD is associated with more severe BPD psychopathology (van den Bosch, Verhuel, and van den Brink 2001), as well as a poor response to psychotherapy (Ryle and Golynkina 1998; Zanarini et al. 2004). Various psychotherapy modalities have been developed for BPD, but these have largely excluded participants with co-occurring AUD from their trials (Linehan et al. 1991; Bateman and Fonagy 1999; Clarkin et al. 2001). Only two small, randomized controlled trials of psychotherapy have been conducted with participants having co-occurring BPD and substance use disorders, each employing fewer than thirty participants and demonstrating only modest efficacy with high dropout rates (Linehan et al. 1999, 2002).

The present study evaluates the efficacy of a form of psycho-dynamic therapy developed specifically for this co-occurring subgroup. Dynamic deconstructive therapy (DDT) involves individual sessions on a weekly basis and is manual-based (Gregory 2003). DDT has goals similar to those of other psychodynamic treatment models (Bateman and Fonagy 2004; Clarkin et al. 2001)

There is currently a lack of adequate investigation into changes individuals make in psychotherapy within more naturalistic settings, particularly for long-term psychodynamic treatment. In addition, psychotherapy outcome researchers have typically pre-screened participants using arguably selective exclusion criteria that limit the generalizability of their findings to “real life” settings. We have designed a research project to help fill this gap.

Gilbert Rose

Gilbert Rose

Gilbert Rose   Distinguished Life Fellow, American Psychiatric Association; Life Member, American Psychoanalytic Association.

Lucy Daniels, a writer and clinical psychologist, is the founder of a nonprofit organization dedicated to fostering emotional and creative freedom through education, outreach, and psychoanalytic treatment and research, and of a psychoanalytically oriented Center for Early Childhood.

Hospitalized for severe anorexia nervosa between the ages of sixteen and twenty-one, she credits psychoanalysis with freeing her from both chronic anorexia nervosa and a debilitating writer's block. Now a prolific writer, she has recently produced two books published at the same time that are related. Dreaming Your Way to Creative Freedom offers a road map for others to use in recognizing shortcomings in creative work and becoming able to observe in dreams the unconscious conflicts that require such shortcomings. The Eyes of the Father is her first novel since Caleb, My Son, a best-seller that won her a Guggenheim Fellowship in literature less than a year after her release from the hospital in 1956.

A gripping story, The Eyes of the Father tells of people who transcend the dead-ends of their past, their gender, and their ethnicity to fashion their own unique—possibly creative—individuality. Its title derives from a verse of William Blake:

This life's dim windows of the soul

Distorts the heavens from pole to pole

And leads you to believe a lie

When you see with, not through, the eye.

One learns that forms of slavery include different ways of being held hostage to the past.

[[RunningHead]]

Building a Bridge Over Troubled Water: Response to Commentaries

Patrick Luyten , Sidney Blatt and Jozef Corveleyn

Jozef Corveleyn

Jozef Corveleyn  

We very much appreciate the thoughtful and thought-provoking comments by Donald Spence and Richard Almond on our target paper. These two comments are all the more noteworthy because they are by two psychoanalysts familiar with both sides of the divide and thus—each in their own way—illustrate the potential to bridge the two cultures within psychoanalysis. Because of the affinity of these commentators with both clinical work and research, however, their remarks also reveal some tension, suggesting that bridging the gap, far from being easy, is a task that will occupy psychoanalysts for years to come. Turning now to the commentaries in specific detail, we begin by addressing Spence's comments, which will allow us to elaborate on the use of case study methods and other procedures to study individual cases systematically, as well as to discuss some additional issues concerning the relevance of psychoanalytic research. Next, we discuss the commentary by Almond, which focuses mainly on problems concerning the translation of psychoanalytic research to clinical practice, and ways to facilitate this process.

Meaning and Method: The Traditional Case Study Method Revisited

Spence eloquently describes the numerous pitfalls associated with the traditional case study method, and points to many of the dangers for psychoanalysis associated with the failure to develop more sophisticated methods of investigation. Needless to say, we fully concur with his plea for alternative, more systematic ways to study individual cases that can enrich the traditional case study method.

Ira Brenner   Panel held at the Winter Meeting, American Psychoanalytic Association, January 23, 2005, New York. Panelists: Vamik Volkan (chair), Norman Itzkowitz, Nancy Hollander.

After the panel on terrorism at the June 2003 meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, it was concluded that a new panel on large-group psychology would promote interest in the value of psychoanalysis applied to world affairs. The idea was accepted by the Program Committee.

Vamik Volkan, Professor Emeritus of Psychiatry and founder of the Center for the Study of Mind and Human Interaction at the University of Virginia and Emeritus Training and Supervising Analyst of the Washington Psychoanalytic Institute, chaired the panel. Joining him were Norman Itzkowitz, Professor Emeritus of Middle Eastern Studies at Princeton University and an Advisory Board member of the Center for the Study of Mind and Human Interaction, and Nancy Hollander, Professor Emeritus of Latin American History at California State University and a faculty member of the Psychoanalytic Center of California.

Volkan presented an introduction using an elegant PowerPoint presentation. The central questions he addressed were: (1) Is there a large-group psychology in its own right? (2) What is a large-group identity? (3) Do neighboring or enemy groups become involved in definable group rituals? (4) What happens to cause a large group to regress? What are the signs and symptoms of such a regression? (5)

977

AffectsStoic Warriors: The Ancient Philosophy Behind the Military Mind. By Nancy Sherman. New York: Oxford University Press, 2005, 256 pp., hardcover £26.00, paperback £15.95.

Stanley Leavy

Stanley Leavy

200 Leeder Hill
Hamden, CT 06517
E-mail: stanleavy@aol.com

Stanley Leavy   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale University (retired); Training and Supervising Analyst, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis (retired).

For a psychoanalyst, stoicism at first reading appears to be a perverse doctrine. Without coming close to the vulgar understanding of the goal of analysis as “self-expression,” or “letting it all hang out,” we do hold to respect for the emotions, at the same time that we try to learn why they may be displaced, submerged, distorted, concealed, exaggerated. For the classical Stoics, emotion is intrinsically inferior to reason, and the sage aims to be free of it.

In this unusual book, Nancy Sherman, University Professor of Philosophy and Adjunct Professor of Law at Georgetown, struggles (that is the word) to demonstrate the approaches of the stoic mentality to the military mind, and its departures from it. Her background is in academic philosophy, but the book has its origins also in her work at the United States Naval Academy at Annapolis, where she was the initial holder of the Distinguished Chair in Ethics. Such formidable qualifications command our attention, especially when she reveals a respectable acquaintance with basic psychoanalytic ideas.

The classical Stoic school of philosophy, of Zeno, Chrysippus, and Epictetus in Greek thought, with Seneca and Marcus Aurelius the principal Roman sources, along with the statesman and orator Cicero, denied the intrinsic reality of the emotions. At most they are to be construed as indicators of cognitions: fear, say, may point to danger, and so be informative, but only virtuous reflection ought be determinative of consequent action. Virtues are the only real good, and we owe happiness to them alone. We are to make the best of all circumstances under the guidance of virtue.

The military character, as understood by the Stoics, stands in its identification with a role assigned to the individual soldier. He (or she, in modern times) has to adjust his personal character to that assignment. If the soldier doesn't yet possess the requisite virtue, he must give the appearance of it. Decorum and etiquette supersede individuality. Even “faking” has its place, as in the instance of the tough sergeant imposing discipline.

From Obstacle To Ally: The Evolution Of Psychoanalytic Practice. By Judith M. Hughes. New York: Brunner-Routledge,2004, 253 pp., $31.95.

Ellen Blumenthal

Ellen Blumenthal

74 Holland Road
Brookline, MA 02445
E-mail: edblumenthal@rcn.com

Ellen Blumenthal   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Instructor in Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School; Associate in Child Psychiatry, Massachusetts General Hospital.

Psychoanalysts and intellectual historians have much in common. Both aim to explore, re-create, and construct a narrative of thoughts and ideas from an earlier life or time. Both use primary sources in their research: the psychoanalyst explores the patient's fantasies, dreams, and associations, and the historian the personal histories, writings, and other communications of the major figures in the story to be told. Finally, both analyst and historian want to uncover new connections in the material that they explore and bring to life in their respective settings. In From Obstacle to Ally: The Evolution of Psychoanalytic Practice, Judith Hughes makes these parallels explicit. She does a masterful job of presenting the history of the clinical theory and practice of psychoanalysis, with the goal of showing how “failures” or technical problems in technique prompted “major advances in psychoanalytic practice” (p. 1).

The book is valuable on many levels. First, it is fun to read. Hughes tells illuminating stories about analysts and their patients and colleagues to illustrate her theory of how technique evolved. Second, she implicitly reminds us where we have been as a profession—both theoretically and technically. Naturally, one must therefore think about where we are headed. Third, From Obstacle to Ally is a valuable teaching text for any course in psychoanalytic history, technique, or clinical theory. Hughes's notes and annotated bibliography are a treasure in themselves.

To tell her stories most effectively, Hughes organizes the book by problems encountered historically in the treatment of five diagnostic categories—hysteria, obsessional neurosis, depression, paranoia, and narcissism. Clinical problems arose in the psychoanalytic treatment of each of these diagnoses, and Hughes describes each obstacle and how it was addressed by analysts who were the “lineal descendents of Freud” (p. 2). She focuses in the first chapter, for example, on the concept of transference as it initially appeared as an obstacle in the treatment of hysterics. She weaves the history of Freud and his relationship with Ferenczi as they struggle with their differing contributions to working with and in the transference and countertransference.

Commentary on Pine's “Psychoanalytic Dictionary”

Eslee Samberg and Elizabeth Auchincloss

Elizabeth Auchincloss

2211 Broadway, Suite 1H
NY, NY 10024
E-mail: ESamberg@aol.com

Elizabeth Auchincloss   Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Professor of Clinical Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College.

In this paper, a distinguished psychoanalyst tackles the problems of how to think and practice coherently in an era of increasing theoretical diversity. In “The Psychoanalytic Dictionary: A Position Paper on Diversity and Its Unifiers,” Fred Pine searches for a metaphor to help him make sense of this new era, experimenting with several possibilities dealing with component parts of a whole. Metaphors of interest to him include “tribes” and “nations,” “pieces of a novel … with a known, main explanatory story line,” a psychoanalytic multiplex theatre with many stages on which the clinical drama unfolds, and the disease model of illness with its various interacting features.

Mostly, however, Pine is intrigued with the metaphor of a psychoanalytic “dictionary,” a compilation of observations, formulations, ideas, concepts, and pieces of theory. The dictionary metaphor leads him naturally to the ancillary metaphor of a “language” to which his dictionary is linked. Pine suggests that we locate psychoanalytic “diversity” in the alphabet and vocabulary of the dictionary and psychoanalytic “unifiers” in the grammar and the core assumptions of the language for which the dictionary is a guide. By working with these linked metaphors, Pine's hope is to create a “conceptual housing” for what he describes as “an increasingly common stance among clinicians who willingly draw from diverse theories as they are applicable to the immediate clinical moment,” as well as for our current state of theory making, in which we operate with “no single [theory that] … fully meets our clinical needs.

[[RunningHead]]

How do we Bridge the Gap? Commentary on Luyten, Blatt, and Corveleyn

Richard Almond

Richard Almond

550 Hamilton Avenue, Suite 339
Palo Alto, CA 94306
E-mail: rjalmond@stanford.edu

Richard Almond   Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute; Adjunct Professor of Psychiatry and Behavioral Science, Stanford University School of Medicine.

The “gap” within psychoanalysis traces its roots to Freud's complex identity as a generalist—a scientist, as well as a physician; a psychologist, but also a writer and humanist. Similarly, psychoanalysis has been located in different places—in the academy, in medical schools, and in autonomous professional networks. As a result there are psychoanalytic researchers, psychoanalytic humanists, and pure psychoanalytic clinicians. Working in different peer settings, answering to different funding sources, the analysts involved have come to speak different languages and hold different values. We are only too familiar with disagreements among analysts around theory; Patrick Luyten, Sidney Blatt, and Jozef Corveleyn draw our attention to another important division—one between the “two cultures” of humanism and science. In psychoanalysis this division takes the form of controversy about the relevance of research on one hand, and the limitations of hermeneutics on the other. Luyten, Blatt, and Coveleyn, analysts and research psychologists, develop and discuss this gap in sophisticated, detailed, and persuasive terms.

I would like to extend their discussion in two ways, influenced by my own perspectives. These perspectives are those of a full-time practitioner doing analysis, therapy, supervision, and teaching, with an earlier career in social psychiatry and a continuing peripheral relation to psychoanalytic research. Each of these experiences leads to a line of response to Luyten, Blatt, and Corveleyn's paper. As an analytic practitioner, I am aware of just how distant the research enterprise and its publications can seem.

611

Duncan Cartwright

15/2 Dines Place

Duncan Cartwright   Senior lecturer, University of Durban-Westville, Durban, South Africa; program manager, Applied Program in Clinical/Counselling Psychology.

The function of autistic defenses in the generation of agoraphobic symptoms is explored in the case of a patient treated in psychoanalytic psychotherapy. The therapist's growing awareness of autistic modes of relating in the case facilitated various changes in the transference relationship. Although oedipal concerns are seen as important, a formulation is presented whereby autistic modes of generating experience are viewed as fundamental to an understanding of agoraphobic experience. Clinical findings in the transference-countertransference relationship led to the additional observation that the retardation of defensive forms of projective identification further contributed to the patient's agoraphobic difficulties. This formulation is tied theoretically to the idea that agoraphobic experience occurs when the dialectic between paranoid-schizoid and autistic-contiguous modes of generating experience collapses. The treatment implications of these observations are briefly explored.

Psychoanalysis in Conjunction with Medication: A Clinical Research Opportunity

Josephine Wright

Josephine Wright

263 West End Avenue, #1-E
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: jowrightmd@aol.com

Josephine Wright   Faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Institute; Director, Child and Adolescent Psychotherapy Program, New York Psychoanalytic Institute; faculty, Berkshire Psychoanalytic Institute.

The increasingly common practice of introducing medication into the analytic relationship is of practical and theoretical concern to analysts. Pharmacological agents and their somatic effects, it is argued, may be seen as a research equivalent to brain lesions, long favored tools in correlating brain with mind function, while psychoanalytic process data may be seen as a fine-tuned instrument for studying the subjective and emotional processes that reflect the underlying brain effect. A method of naturalistic study of psychoanalytic process both with and without a psychopharmacological agent Is described and illustrated in two patients. The potential of this method for providing data useful in understanding the mechanism of action of psychoactive medications and of individual variations in response is explored. Both patients, treated with stimulant for ADHD, showed striking differences in self- and object representation, defense, affect, and cognition that correlated with analytic periods on and off the medication. The implications of these observations for understanding both the action of the medication and the fundamental functional disruptions in the condition are explored as well.

Psychoanalysis and the Art of the Mentally Ill

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

115 East 86th Street
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: ahesman@earthlink.net

Aaron Esman   Professor of Clinical Psychiatry (emeritus), Weill Cornell Medical College; faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Institute and Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Fortune's Favorite Child: The Uneasy Life of Walter Anderson. By Christopher Maurer. Jackson: University Press of Mississippi, 2004, xxiii + 367 pp., $35.00.

The Discovery of the Art of the Insane. By John M. MacGregor. Princeton: Princeton University Press, 1989, xix + 390 pp.

Artistry of the Mentally Ill. By Hans Prinzhorn (1921). Translated by Eric von Brockdorff. New York: Springer Verlag, 1972, xxi + 274 pp.

A Mental Patient as Artist: Adolf Wölfli. By Walter Morgenthaler (1922). Published as Madness and Art: The Life and Work of Adolf Wölfli. Translated and edited by Aaron H Esman. Lincoln: University of Nebraska Press, 1992, xvii + 131 pp.

Psychoanalytic Explorations in Art. By Ernst Kris. New York: International Universities Press, 1952, 385 pp.

The recent publication of Fortune's Favorite Child by Christopher Maurer raises again the old question of the relation between creativity, particularly in the visual arts, and mental illness-or, in the literature of an earlier era, “madness.” The issue, which goes back at least to the days of Plato and Aristotle, has enjoyed particular currency in recent years because of the rise in popular interest in so-called “outsider art” or, in the term fashioned by the French painter Jean Dubuffet, “Art Brut” (“raw art”). Although the scope of the designation “outsider” tends to be somewhat loose and broad, the term refers particularly to persons who are self-taught, outside the mainstream of what Dubuffet called “the art of the museums”; often eccentric and isolated, they are in many instances psychotic inhabitants of psychiatric institutions.

645

Thoughts on Medication and Psychoanalysis: A Lay Analyst's View

Wendy Olesker

Wendy Olesker

15 West 72nd Street

Wendy Olesker   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute; Adjunct Clinical Associate Professor of Psychology and Supervisor, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

The issues involved in split analytic treatments—where a second person manages the patient's medication—are discussed from the point of view of a developmentalist and lay analyst. Case material is presented to illustrate the interplay of medication with other elements of the psychoanalytic situation. Medication and its effects, it is argued, should be accorded no special status apart from other interventions and enactments in an analysis. Some see medication and psychoanalysis as parallel processes, two separate and unintegrated theoretical systems, and recommend shifting back and forth between models of the mind or becoming “bilingual”; against this view, it is argued that anything the analyst does will affect the patient's thoughts, fantasies, and even physiology in individual ways, and only attention to analytic material can reveal what an intervention means in a specific case. Success in split treatment depends on a collaborative therapeutic alliance among patient, analyst, and consultant. Because there is as yet no theory that bridges psychoanalysis and psychopharmacology, analysts must talk of these matters as incompletely synthesized and regard them as part of the challenges that make psychoanalysis the exciting, impossible profession it is.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Masculinity in the Twenty-First Century: An Introduction

Janice Lieberman

Janice Lieberman

55 East 87th Street Suite 2J
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: janicelieberman@compuserve.com

Janice Lieberman  

For the past forty years or more the psychoanalytic literature on gender and sexuality has focused almost exclusively on female psychology and female development. Male psychology has for the most part been neglected, with the exception of a few scattered contributions (e.g., Abelin 1975; Alizade 2003; Breen 1993; Diamond 2004; Fogel, Lane, and Liebert 1986; Fogel 1998; Herzog 2001; Ross 1979; Stoller 1965; Trowell and Etchegoyen 2002).

Journals devoted to the subject of gender have privileged studies of femininity and of gays and lesbians. With this special section on masculinity we hope to begin to remedy this situation by discussing and debating contemporary gender theory and by examining contemporary clinical cases of men in psychoanalysis.

What is masculinity in 2006? If notions of masculinity have changed, just how have they changed in the century since Freud (1905) wrote Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality? Is there one masculinity or many (just as there may be many femininities)? The answers to these questions are so complex that some thinkers consider the very concept of gender to be irrelevant. At one extreme is the Scottish sociologist John MacInnes (1998), who in The End of Masculinity: The Confusion of Sexual Genesis and Sexual Difference in Modern Society concluded that it is impossible to define “masculinity” because “no such thing exists.” He claims that one cannot say merely that masculinity is now in crisis, because “the briefest historical survey will show that masculinity has always been in one crisis or another” (p. 11). Adrienne Harris (2005), less strident, sees gender as “soft assembly” and views it through the lens of chaos theory. For Harris the body expresses historical and social forces that affect its “gait, mien, posture, body type, the outcome of nutritional specificity, diet, aesthetics of dress and body presentation” (p. 121).

1059

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Action as Ejection

Daisy Franco

Daisy Franco

50 East 72nd Street, Apt. 8C
New York, NY 10021

Daisy Franco   Training and Supervising Analyst, Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research (IPTAR), New York.

The systematic analysis of acting-out episodes can be used in assessing analytic progress. Variables to be considered are the nature of the wish, the type of defense, and the degree of concreteness (versus symbolization) of the mental processes used in attempting actualization (as distinct from the resort to action). Two acting-out episodes of a borderline patient who acted out as a character trait, both occurring outside the analytic setting, are presented as illustrations. In the first one, occurring relatively early in the analysis, when split-off negative and positive self-images had to be rigidly maintained, ejection of the negative self-image was actualized via the regressive use of a symbolic equation and the mechanism of displacement, obliterating the distinction between an internal feeling and an external thing that here was literally thrown out. The later episode, occurring after the split was healed and within the context of a frustrating heterosexual involvement, contained an acted-out allusion to identification and competition with the mother. As in a dream, via associations, an unconscious wish for oedipal victory was revealed. Whereas in the first episode the goal of ejection was central, with splitting and denial the underlying defenses, it was absent from the second, in which an attempt was made to actualize a repressed infantile wish and made greater use of symbolization. It is concluded that acting-out episodes at different periods of the analysis, when systematically analyzed, can serve in assessing a patient's progress.

[[RunningHead]]

Biography and Creativity

Virgini Woolf: On Becoming a Writer. By Katherine Dalsimer. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2001. pp. 206. pp. $30.00..

E. Dahl

E. Dahl

Fax: 203-397-2943
E-mail: kdahl23@comcast.net

E. Dahl   Training and Supervising Analyst and Chair, Child Analysis Program, Western New England Institute of Psychoanalysis.

Virginia Woolf was haunted by her mother, Julia Stephens, who died when Virginia was thirteen. We are haunted by Virginia Woolf, one of the giants of modernism in fiction, concerned with the themes of time, memory, and the interiority of women. In 2004 alone there were nine books, 143 scholarly articles, and thirteen dissertations on the subject of Virginia Woolf (Hussey 2004). Scholarly focus ranges from her aesthetic contribution to modernism through the use of such devices as stream-of-consciousness interior monologues; her “poetics of time” her feminism; her exploration of mourning and the Great War; and the relation between her manic-depressive illness and her creativity. Not only was Woolf a prolific writer of novels and critical essays; she also began to keep a journal in her early teens that she continued until her death at fifty-nine; thirty volumes of her diaries and four thousand letters have provided an unusually intimate window on her inner world and her development as a writer. Katherine Dalsimer, in her elegant book, Virginia Woolf: On Becoming a Writer, “listens” to Woolf's diaries and published writings as a careful analyst might listen to the material in a good hour. Dalsimer “hears” in the silences, the elisions, and the associations in Woolf's texts how her experience, particularly with loss and death, informed her development as a writer as she struggled to keep vibrant her memories of those she had lost: “Perhaps the experience of early loss makes memory itself a matter of urgent concern. It is memory that maintains our living connection with the dead; it is the most palpable way to preserve what is otherwise irrevocably lost” (p. xv).

Dalsimer opens with Woolf's statement, “We think back through our mothers if we are women,” weaving a discussion of To the Light house intos an exploration of the writer's relationship with her mother. Virginia had her first breakdown shortly after her mother's death.

679

Sigmund Freud-Max Eitingon Briefwechsel1906-1939 (The correspondence of Sigmund Freud and Max Eitingon 1906-1939). Edited by Michael Schröter. 2 vols. Tübingen: Edition Diskord, 2004, 1050 pp., £ 77.00.

Eran Rolnik

Eran Rolnik

226 Hayarkon Street
Tel-Aviv
ISRAEL 63405
E-mail: rolnik@post.tau.ac.il

Eran Rolnik   Candidate, Israel Psychoanalytic Society; Postdoctoral Research Fellow, Stephen Roth Institute for the Study of Contemporary Anti-Semitism and Racism, Tel-Aviv University.

Students and historians of psychoanalysis are often reminded that familiarizing themselves with Freud's vast correspondence (estimated at thirty thousand letters) can be as rewarding to the understanding of his teachings as the reading of his published essays. Surely, at the turn of the twenty-first century, the declining art of letter writing makes this particular genre unfamiliar to many. Those who cherish this source of knowledge, however, will find these two volumes as good as the best any Freud reader could find.

Freud's correspondence with Max Eitingon spanned a period of thirty years. It is probably the last remaining voluminous exchange of letters with a single disciple, a member of the notorious “Secret Committee,” whose place in the annals of psychoanalysis is often misrepresented and underestimated.

Reading this correspondence we get a firsthand account of the formative years of the psychoanalytic movement: the founding of the first psychoanalytic day clinic and training institute in Berlin; the establishment of the International Training Committee; the bitter disputes over Wilhelm Reich's political leanings; the painful technical controversies with Ferenczi and Rank.

In April 1933 the possibility that Eitingon might have to leave Germany was first raised. After forty years of living in Germany, the Russian-born Eitingon emphasized his complete identification with the institute he had founded, and his determination to leave Berlin only if forced to do so. Nevertheless, Aryanization advanced rapidly; two of Eitingon's non-Jewish colleagues hurried to Vienna so that Freud might appoint them to be the legal successors to Eitingon, and on August 23, 1933, Freud wrote Ernest Jones, “Berlin is lost” (Freud and Jones 1993, p. 726).

1033

[[RunningHead]]

Masculinity and Its Discontents: Commentary on Reichbart and Diamond

Jane Flax

Jane Flax

2129 Florida Avenue NW #504
Washington, DC 20008
Fax: 202-332-4731
E-mail: jane.flax@worldnet.att.net

Jane Flax   Professor, Department of Political Science, Howard University; psychotherapist in private practice, Washington, DC.

Since the inception of psychoanalysis, gender, sexuality, identity, and sexual difference have been vexed-and vexing-topics. As the tortured footnotes of his Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality (1905) and later writings indicate, Freud continued to puzzle about these matters. Subsequently, these terms remain the site of recurring and frequently bitter disputes among psychoanalysts. Often these battles concern “the woman question”—what/who she is or wants-and what “healthy” femininity entails. Richard Reichbart and Michael Diamond encourage us to render narratives about masculinity equally problematic. Each critiques a paradigm, still dominant within psychoanalysis, of the development of masculinity-disidentif ication with the mother. They both suggest more fluid and complex notions of masculinity. Each author also claims that an adequate understanding of gender identity requires more attention to cultural factors than is often accorded them in psychoanalytic work. However, neither Diamond nor Reichbart goes nearly far enough in opening up ways to rethink masculinity and gender identity.

Diamond and Reichbart offer interesting insights into masculinity and its vicissitudes and present compelling arguments against the disidentification model. They correctly argue that this model is founded on a rigid and dichotomous notion of gender. Intrinsic to the logic of disidentification is the assumption that gender is a binary system in which there are only two positions; one belongs to one gender to the extent to which one does not belong to the other. Thus, within this system, since his first identification is with a female (his mother), the boy to be male must first become not female.

1131

[[RunningHead]]

Summaries of the Fifth Annual Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association

Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser

E-mail: shauser@jbcc.harvard.edu

Stuart Hauser  

A firm and encouraging sign of the laudatory impact of earlier poster sessions is the increased breadth and number of excellent contributions—from many countries and disciplines—in this, the fifth year the sessions have been held. In 2005, our expanding, integrative review committee could select from the largest number of submissions ever received. Our enhanced review committee—representing adult and child psychoanalysis, clinical psychology, developmental psychology, anthropology, and history—chose twenty-eight posters, displayed and energetically discussed at the 2006 Winter Meeting. These presentations are notable for their clear communication of conceptualizations, research approaches, and interpretations of findings; for their interfaces with biological, psychological, and psychiatric perspectives; and for a diversity of theoretical frameworks.

In light of the large number of accepted presentations, for the first time JAPA will publish the posters in two successive issues. We have chosen to begin publishing summaries (rather than telegraphic abstracts), in order to provide readers greater access to an investigator's thinking. While likely not as effective as the lively and informative conversations that occur with the researchers in front of their posters, these more elaborated reports should lead patient readers to a fuller grasp of the topic a poster is addressing (e.g., “Measuring Transference Phenomena with fMRI”). Moreover, through their excellent bibliographies, these summaries enable still deeper perusal of a poster's constructs, central themes, and empirical methods.

1299

Richard Munich   Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Anyone who has hated or who has worked with someone who has hated will find this book provocative and potentially useful. It is a difficult and intelligent collection of fourteen essays by various authors who the editor hopes will renew psychoanalysts' interest in some of our more common prejudices and social trends. The book is not about blaming a dysfunctional world; rather, its subject is the internal sources of hate that are abundantly reinforced by an external climate of toxicity that appears undiminished despite advances in many social assumptions and endeavors.

Affect regulation, once subsumed under the rubric of the autonomous ego functions, is now a topic of its own for psychoanalysts and psychotherapists of many persuasions. Given our daily news, hate is an affect that could undoubtedly use more widespread regulation, but first it must be understood. It may not be easy to sit so long, reading so much about hate, but these essays place new emphasis on our aggressive impulses and devalued objects.

In the editor's introduction, Donald Moss, who has other essays in the collection, lays the groundwork for the hypothesis that a way of managing unbearable identifications and yearnings is essentially to renounce them by converting “I want” or “I fear” first into “I hate” and finally into “We hate.

1403

Clinical Theory

Relational Psychoanalysis: Volume 2. Innovation and Expansion. Relational Psychoanalysis Book Series, Vol. 28. Edited by Lewis Aron and Adrienne Harris. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2005, 512 pp., £49.95.

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards

200 East 89th Street
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-427-0585
E-mail: arniedr@aol.com

Arnold Richards   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute; Editor Emeritus, Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

This book is the second volume in a series presenting what its editors, Lewis Aron and Adrienne Harris, see as an emerging new tradition in psychoanalysis. The first volume, Relational Psychoanalysis: The Emergence of a Tradition, edited by Stephen Mitchell and Lewis Aron, was published in 1999. In their introduction to the second volume, published in 2005, the editors describe the six-year period between the publication of the two collections as one of “great promise and a great loss within the relational and wider analytic community.” The death of Stephen Mitchell shortly after the completion of the first volume forms the sad backdrop for the publication of this one.

Wayne C. Booth (1979) offers the following oath to be taken by book reviewers: “I will publish nothing favorable or unfavorable about books or articles that I have not read through at least once. I will try to publish nothing about any book … until I have understood it, which is to think I can give an account that the author himself will recognize as just. Any attempt at overstanding will follow this initial act of attempted respect” (quoted in Patai and Corral 2004, p. 688). I needed to keep this caveat in mind, because I do not come to the subject of this book, relational psychoanalysis, as a first-time commentator. I have authored or coauthored reviews of important relational authors (Bachant and Richards 1993; Richards 1999) and engaged in a sharp and perhaps somewhat notorious interchange with Stephen Mitchell himself in the pages of the Division 39 Section 1 newsletter, the Round Robin. But I have tried here to live up to Booth's principles; I read the anthology cover to cover three times, and have done my best to tackle each paper on its author's terms, and to consider as well the editors' intentions in putting together this particular collection.

1413

[[RunningHead]]

Perspectives on Psychoanalysis

Impossible Training: Arelational View Of Psychoanalytic Education Vol. 26. By Emanuel Berman. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2004. 296 pp., $39.95.

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg

21 Bloomingdale Road
White Plains, NY 10605
Fax: 914-997-5997
E-mail: Okernber@med.cornell.edu

Otto Kernberg   Director, Personality Disorders Institute, New York Presbyterian Hospital, Westchester Division; Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Medical College of Cornell University; Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

In recent times, new voices have expressed a growing concern with serious problems in psychoanalytic education. A sharp critique is evolving regarding the rigidity and authoritarianism prevalent in psychoanalytic institutes, their tendency to induce a subservient conventionalism and a combination of idealization of some preferred teachers, local idols, or psychoanalytic orientation, while ignoring or denigrating others. The contemporary development of alternative theoretical psychoanalytic formulations and technical approaches stands in contrast, for those authors, to the prevalent routine transmission of traditional sources of information, and the unwarranted lack of excitement of the intellectual life of psychoanalytic institutions. A central issue of these critiques is the inordinate power vested in the training analysis system, and the organizational distortions arising from that dysfunctional power distribution. Reeger has expressed that critique from a Norwegian experience, Garza-Guerrero from a Mexican background, and Meyer from a Brazilian perspective. Now we have this interesting text from Emanuel Berman, presenting the experience of the Israeli Psychoanalytic Society in dealing with some of these important concerns, from the perspective of the relational school of psychoanalysis.

A central thesis of Berman's book is that the present problems of psychoanalytic education derive from an ideological conflict that runs throughout the entire history of psychoanalytic institutions.

281

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mothers and Daughters

Kay Reed

Kay Reed

349 Lancaster Avenue
Haverford, PA 19041
E-mail: kayreed2@msn.com

Kay Reed  

Ruth Fischer introduced the panel, noting that in recent years many of our ideas about female development have been modified, or even drastically altered. The concept of primary femaleness has supplanted the long held conviction that the girl's sense of herself as female begins with an awareness of genital anatomic difference. The female triangulation conflict has been seriously reconsidered. We have a new appreciation of the impact of the girl's body on the organization of her development and have begun to delineate the differences in male and female development. As we listen to patients, it is with a new appreciation of countertransference, the patient's point of view, and intersubjectivity.

Amid this change, our ideas about the mother-daughter relationship have remained intact: a tenacious bond that eventually needs to be broken for development to progress. We have traditionally postulated a relatedness/autonomy dichotomy, with the girl oscillating between happy union with her mother and her own self-expression, unable to express independent initiative and mature genital sexuality without breaking the bond.

Today, however, we realize that development is not purely linear and internally driven; we acknowledge relational components and anticipate evolution over time. The goal is no longer the severing of the tie to mother (in order to attain separation, autonomy, and mature sexuality), but an evolving transformation of the relationship (to allow the time needed for integration and identification). Disengagement is replaced by the consolidation of new internalizations, resulting in new levels of organization and enrichment, not disruption or abandonment. It is the lack of the modification of this bond that can lead to problems. This is a radical innovation in our conceptualization of female psychology.

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, San Francisco, June 26, 2004.

Panelists: Ruth Fischer (chair), Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel, Ethel Spector Person, Barbara S. Shapiro, Lynne Zeavin.

[[RunningHead]]

Melanie Klein: Matricide as Pain and Creativity. By Julia Kristeva, Translated by Ross Guberman. New York: Columbia University Press, 2001. pp. 296. pp. $19.95..

Alan Bass

Alan Bass

27 West 96th Street (#1F)
New York, NY 10025
E-mail: bassaj@aol.com

Alan Bass   Training Analyst, IPTAR, New York Freudian Society; Adjunct Professor of Philosophy and Psychoanalysis, The Graduate Faculty, New School University.

Julia Kristeva's Melanie Klein operates on three levels. It is a study of the life, work, and influence of Klein; it is the second volume of a trilogy on female genius, whose other subjects are Hannah Arendt and Colette; and it is an expansion of Kristeva's own thinking. On the first level the book is excellent, but it is troubling on the second and third.

Kristeva gives a fine, compact overview of the evolution of Klein's thought in relation to her personal history and political battles. The portrait of Klein as the inventor of child analysis, original thinker, center of the Controversial Discussions, daughter of a difficult mother and mother of a difficult daughter, possessive and envious leader of a group, will be familiar to readers of the Grosskurth biography. But Kristeva has a distinct take on Klein, encapsulated in the book's subtitle: Matricide as Pain and Creativity.

On the first level Kristeva reads Klein in relation to what she sees as the central idea of psychoanalysis: “the human soul, which is bound up with the body and language … can become a privileged realm of rebirth” (p. 6). Where Freud concentrated on the child's desires and repressions, Klein focused on the infant's pain and splitting. This pain is object-related from the outset and is a manifestation of the death drive. The result is Klein's famous emphasis on aggression in the mother-infant relation. But such aggression is also the conflicted ground of symbolization, says Kristeva, putting “matricide … along with envy and gratitude, at the origin of our capacity to think” (pp. 12-13). Klein had “the courage to proclaim, in her own way, and without equivocation” that “in order to think, one must first lose the mother” (pp. 130-131). As the origin of symbolization, matricide is a kind of rebirth.

Kristeva's conception of rebirth serves to expand Klein's idea that “both sexes experience a primary feminine phase …” (p. 118). All infants have oral-sadistic incorporative fantasies about a “breast that includes the penis” (p. 118), that is, fantasies about taking into one's own body what is inside the mother's body.

[[RunningHead]]

The Riddle of Masculinity

Mary Brady

Mary Brady

San Francisco, CA
Fax: 415-346-5213
E-mail: mbrady66@earthlink.net

Mary Brady  

This panel was born out of Nancy Chodorow's observation that the “riddle of masculinity,” has not generated the extensive thought that has been accorded the “riddle of femininity” (her reference was to Freud's famous remark in 1933 that psychology is “unable to solve the riddle of femininity”). Maleness has been seen as the norm, and thus unnecessary to think about. Chodorow pointed out that Freud's writings, and those of his colleagues, which assumed a male norm and assumed that the penis (present, absent, or castrated) is central to gender and sexuality for both sexes, have received extensive criticism and revision. Indeed, the “riddle of femininity” has occasioned wide-ranging theoretical work. Writings on female psychology have expanded our understanding of the oedipus complex, preoedipal phases, psycho-sexual development, superego formation, bisexuality, homosexuality, and heterosexuality. A “matricentric” focus has contributed to our understanding of the early mother-child relation and led to relational psychoanalysis, the elaboration of Kleinian and object relations theories, attention to nonverbal elements in the analytic space, and the characterization of analysis as unfolding in the transitional realm. Our understanding of female psychology has deepened and gained in nuance and complexity. Meanwhile, the “riddle of masculinity” remains largely unremarked and unaddressed.

Chodorow expressed indebtedness to the studies of colleagues who have described homosexual boyhoods, alternative masculinities, and the becoming-gay boy's relations to his mother and father. By comparison and implication these studies have also delineated patterns of typical heterosexual masculinities.

Chodorow asked the panelists to problematize their understanding of masculinity by responding to three central questions. All three

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 22, 2005.

Panelists: Nancy Chodorow (chair), Gerald Fogel, Ethel Spector Person, Bennett Simon.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pluralism: An Introduction

Ephi Betan

Ephi Betan

980 Hammond Drive, Suite 100
Atlanta, GA 30328
E-mail: ejbet@aol.com

Ephi Betan   Emory University Psychoanalytic Institute; Georgia School of Professional Psychology, Argosy University/Atlanta.

In all affairs it's a healthy thing now and then to hang a question mark on the things you have long taken for granted.

—Bertrand Russell

Where would we be today if Freud had not challenged the assumptions of his day regarding human behavior and had never made a project of exploring the deeper meanings of psychological existence? Although I recognize that Freud's search for truth in an overarching theory explaining all psychic phenomena stands in contrast, epistemologically, to our contemporary, postmodern idea that objective knowledge divorced from subjectivity and context is an illusion, I still wonder whether psychoanalysis has not, in a way, come full circle. What we share with Freud and every analyst following in his footsteps is a desire to make sense of the puzzling nature of the psychological. We also share with Freud a reluctance to foreclose knowledge. Theoretical diversity has been inherent in psychoanalysis from early on. Freud's writings stimulated many others? explorations of human behavior, and the outcome has been a mixture of theories influenced by each individual's perspective, history, and personality. Different and diverse constructions of psychoanalytic knowledge characterize our schools of thought. Psychoanalysis began with a burgeoning of multiple perspectives, perhaps stagnated in the competition to hold the “truth,” and now has returned to the kind of vitality that accompanies exploration and intellectual creativity.

Why, then, does it seem like we are in such a different place? Pluralism is more than just acknowledging multiple perspectives.

357

[[RunningHead]]

Expanding the Evidence Base for the Cost-Effectiveness of Long-Term Psychoanalytic Treatment

Jolien Zevalkink and Caspar Berghout

Caspar Berghout

Olympiaplein 4
1076 AB Amsterdam
Netherlands
E-mail: zevalkink@npsai.nl

Caspar Berghout  

A survey in 1943 by Obendorf was oriented toward answering the multiple question “What type of treatment is best suited to what kind of patient, suffering from what kind of illness, at what point in life, when treated by what kind of analyst, in what manner?” (Galatzer-Levy et al. 2000, p. 53). In recent years, the question of the cost-effectiveness of psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy can be added to these concerns. Despite this long history of posing the right questions, research into the effectiveness of psychoanalytic treatment has been hampered by vexing methodological, technical, theoretical, and ethical 1313 problems, not to mention a diverse array of opposing viewpoints on scientific and empirical research itself (Gerber 2004). Nevertheless, we consider systematic empirical research relevant to the study of psychoanalysis. To overcome some of the daunting difficulties in this area, it is important to choose a theoretical framework, research instruments, and a research design adequate to addressing these questions. Our aim here is to explain the choice of theoretical framework, instruments, and design of a study into the effectiveness of psychoanalytic treatment for which data collection started in January 2005. The project is a collaboration of four mental health institutes and is intended to evaluate the health- and cost-effectiveness of ambulatory government-funded psychoanalytic psychotherapy and psychoanalysis in regular clinical practice in the Netherlands. This will be done by using a routine outcome monitoring system developed for monitoring change in long-term psychotherapeutic treatments. In addition, the project will evaluate the usefulness of the monitoring system for quality assurance purposes.

Theoretical Framework

A theoretical framework accompanying research on the effectiveness of psychoanalytic treatment should provide hypotheses about areas in which patients might benefit from the treatment.

1313

Controversy

Understanding Dissidence and Controversy in the History of Psychoanalysis. Edited by Martin S. Bergmann. New York: Other Press, 2004, 396 pp., £28.00.

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

37 Woodsome Road
Dartmouth Park, London
NW5 1SA
UNITED KINGDOM
E-mail: d.tuckett@ucl.ac.uk

David Tuckett   Training and Supervising Analyst, British Psychoanalytical Society; Chair, European Psychoanalytic Federation Working Party on Comparative Clinical Methods.

This book, the vision of its editor and primary contributor, Martin Bergmann, comprises a lengthy position paper, eight discussion papers authored by well-known psychoanalysts, and the transcript of the discussion that took place between them and three representatives of the sponsoring trust, at a specially invited conference on two days in February 2003.

For me the central contribution of the book is by Bergmann, who celebrated his ninetieth birthday on the conference's opening day. His cogent and original 110-page opening paper, supplemented by a later chapter on the British psychoanalyst Charles Rycroft, is perspicacious and lucid. Its aim is to use detailed understanding of controversies and instead of deploring them to search “for a way for them to deepen our understanding of the nature of psychoanalysis” (p. 87). Reading this volume did achieve that for me, although here I can give only a bald summary.

The minutes of the proceedings of the Vienna Society suggest that the first dissident, Adler, neither changed his own view nor took on any of Freud's thinking about the human mind at any stage. Close study suggests the difference between them was always there but took time to be realized. In fact, Adler always saw aggression as the origin of anxiety and never accepted (or perhaps never truly understood) the idea of unconscious repression. He learned from Freud the technique of listening to what patients say and letting them speak freely about their lives and thoughts, but what he did and thought with what he heard was very different. All this only became clear to Freud in 1911, when in response to an Adler paper he pronounced, “This is not psychoanalysis” (p. 10). “Instead of presenting,” says Bergmann, a “psychology of the unconscious", Adler advocated “surface ego psychology?” (p. 10).

1393
