[[RunningHead]]

The Telescoping of Generations: Listening to the Narcissistic Links Between Generations. By Hayde Faimberg. New Library of Psychoanalysis Series. New York: Brunner-Routledge, 2005, 156 pp., hardcover £87.50, paperback £33.95.

Manuel Furer

Manuel Furer

166 East 93rd Street
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-987-8133
E-mail: mfmdpc@yahoo.com

Manuel Furer   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

This book is a collection of previously published papers that reflect Hayde Faimberg's changing views about psychoanalytic theory and its application to clinical work over twenty-five years. It is worth noting that her interests are again current, as an increasing number of investigators and clinicians are attempting to put the explication of earliest psychic functioning into the forefront of our understanding of the therapeutic effect in psychoanalysis.

1431

Faimberg is convinced of the correctness of Freud's hypothesis in “Instincts and Their Vicissitudes” that in the beginning we function as purified “pleasure egos” (1915, p. 136). What is pleasurable (tension-reducing) is the I, and what is unpleasurable (tension-increasing) is the outer world. He consequently concluded that “at the very beginning, it seems the external world, objects, and what is hated are identical” (p. 136). Faimberg's contribution is to add that primitive object relations are established at that same period and the combination remains as an unchanged position until analyzed. In her theory of narcissistic functioning in early life, she posits a lived contradiction, since there is both a sense of self-sufficiency and yet the need to confirm that self-sufficiency in the child via the other.

In each generation, psychic functioning of the child activates the same narcissistic functioning in the parent, and the two interact psychically. The child as the other is to the parent, as the other is to the hypothetical child, the hated other. For the child to attempt to differentiate himself from the mother results in her hatred of the child as the other. Pathological psychic functioning results when the child identifies with the other of the parent. Since the content of the parents' ideas and feelings are about their own parents, there is a “telescoping of generations.”

What one finds lacking in Faimberg's explanations is a place for human motivation, particularly as a participant in psychic conflict. In some of her illustrations, the latter seems to be necessary, as in object sparing or disavowal of a pregnancy to avoid the pain of jealousy, but there is no generalization of motivation as an explanatory concept.

Theodore Jacobs   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Albert Einstein College of Medicine; Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute and NYU Psychoanalytic Institute.

It is accepted wisdom that book reviewers, like analysts, should strive for evenhanded objectivity, judging a book according to well-recognized literary or academic standards.

Since by even the most flexible of measures I fall woefully short of that admirable goal in writing this review, I take some comfort in the well-known fact that reviewers, again like analysts, fail as often as they succeed in attaining objectivity, and, like those colleagues who breathe a sigh of relief when, privately, they acknowledge that the ideal of neutrality regularly eludes them, I hope to ease my conscience by confessing that what I have to say about James McLaughlin's wonderful book, The Healer's Bent: Solitude and Dialogue in the Clinical Encounter, lacks the barest hint of objectivity.

I blame this shortcoming on the book review editor, who, knowing that I share McLaughlin's way of thinking and working, asked me to review his book anyway. What she did not know is that for many years, ever since I read his early papers in the seventies, Jim McLaughlin has been my ideal and my personal hero.

Jim's book, a memorable collection of writing, new and old, reassures me that my initial view of him as one of the most innovative'and important'contributors to our field was not composed solely of transference.

1422

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Creation and Social Transmission of Psychoanalytic Knowledge: Commentary on Richards

Paul Roazen

Paul Roazen

Paul Roazen   Professor Emeritus, Political and Social Science, York University, Toronto, Ontario; Adjunct Professor, Department of Psychiatry, Tufts University School of Medicine. Dr. Roazen recently published Edoardo Weiss: The House that Freud Built, reviewed in this issue's Book section. Paul Roazen passed away November 3, 2005.

Those of us who have spent our lives largely within academic institutions have to be appreciative of efforts, like those of Arnold Richards, to expand the horizons of organized psychoanalysis. But universities are not themselves exempt from many of the same cultural pressures that affect organizations set up for the training and transmission of psychoanalysis, and are also subject to the disparity between glittering historical ideals and disappointing concrete realities. And valuable insights from the sociology of knowledge are as relevant to understanding academic life as to psychoanalysis.

In keeping with Freud's unusual penchant for reminding readers about landmarks in the development of his work, psychoanalysts have displayed a special and abiding interest in the past literature of their field. Professional papers regularly rehearse the previous literature on the subjects being discussed. From my own perspective as a student of intellectual history, however, old controversies have a way of getting sacrificed, through being truncated, for the sake of today's needs; and therefore the fullest possible scope of the history of ideas has a way of losing out to the immediate demands of contemporary crises.

Let me move immediately to the issue of A. A. Brill's central part in the American reception of psychoanalysis, since this is of special concern to Richards.

389

All ideologies may become, in Dostoevsky's metaphor, “demons,” “devils,” “bèssy,” — evil spirits that drive into a flock of swine and hurl them into the water to drown themselves. Yet ideology in the form of poisonous prejudice against race, class, religion, ethnic minority, or gender is often skillfully hidden behind deception and lies. In John Milton's words, it is “a certain clandestine Hostility covered over with the name of Peace.” Yawning abysses open up between words and deeds. These “Big Lies” then effectively sway many well-meaning, but naive, people.

An inquiry into the motivations of these ideologies shows that it is not so much what the hated one does that counts, but what he is.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Free Clinics: Psychoanalysis and Social Justice, 1918-1938. By Elizabeth Ann Danto. New York: Columbia University Press, 2005, 352 pp., $29.50.

Paul Roazen

Paul Roazen

Paul Roazen   Late Professor Emeritus, Political and Social Science, York University, Toronto.

This is a thoroughly amiable inquiry into a little-known aspect of the history of psychoanalysis, those free clinical facilities that were associated with the original European psychoanalytic organizations. Elizabeth Danto, its author, teaches at the Hunter College School of Social Work, and has an admirable set of progressive political convictions.

With the microscope of left-wing politics in hand, Danto does a superb job of recounting all the details connected with this relatively unknown story. Following a suggestion by Freud in 1918, the Berlin Psychoanalytic Institute, supported by Max Eitingon's money, succeeded in establishing the most extensively well-endowed set of facilities. But in Vienna, London, and Budapest, psychoanalysts followed suit. These free psychoanalytic treatment centers eventually came to exist in some ten cities and seven countries.

It seems to me that one of the most admirably broad-minded things about Danto's book is that she includes Alfred Adler's Viennese clinics as part of the tale of these humanitarian efforts within psychoanalysis. She even refers to “the psychoanalyst Alfred Adler” (p. 24), as if he had never been expelled by Freud. Within Vienna, Adler's child guidance clinics were supported by the Social Democratic government, a fact that made Adler more famous there than Freud himself.

It remains unknown exactly how Freudians adapted traditional psychoanalytic technique in order to reach patients from the lower classes, but doubtless changes had to be made (if not publicly acknowledged) in how analysts approached this new set of clients. Hundreds of patients could be regularly involved.

1036

[[RunningHead]]

The Concept of the Negative in Psychoanalysis

Commentary on Wilson

Lewis Kirshner

Lewis Kirshner

E-mail: lewis_kirshner@hms.harvard.edu

Lewis Kirshner   Associate Clinical Professor, Harvard Medical School; Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute.

Mitchell Wilson's rich and complex paper reflects the growing interest of American psychoanalysts in perspectives outside the familiar ego psychology and object relations approaches in which we were trained. I believe it marks a dividing point in the evolution of psychoanalysis, one in which the divergence has become more pronounced between those who retain a strong commitment to a natural science model of mental function, linked with evolutionary biology, developmental research, and the neurosciences, and those turning to a hermeneutic, linguistic approach growing out of a philosophical literature on subjectivity. For a long time, this bifurcation, first elaborated by Paul Ricoeur (1961-1962) as the separate domains of force and meaning, was uneasily contained within the Freudian project. In Wilson's terms, the zone of potentially objective knowledge existed in dialectical tension with the realm of uncertainty and the unknown. Even though the ego psychologists' goal of identifying the causal mechanisms of the neuroses by a study of the vicissitudes of early (especially oedipal) conflicts was quietly retired some years ago, the objective of connecting adult symptoms to specific failures of early development has persisted. It would be satisfying to identify unconscious incestuous wishes that underlie neurotic problems in success or sexual relationships, for example, or to locate parental deficits in empathy, attunement, or attachment that have led to specific adult emotional problems. Needless to say, one can always find evidence for these and other plausible etiological factors in every analytic case.

423

This book opens with a compelling question: To whom does the twentieth century belong—Oedipus or Electra? The author, Jill Scott, a scholar of German at Queen's University, Ontario, sets out to demonstrate that the myth of Electra appeals to modern writers in ways the myth of Oedipus does not. The result is an interesting study of the creative uses of the myth by a varied group of writers, some relatively unfamiliar, as well as a look at their perceptions of psychoanalysis.

[[RunningHead]]

Where Does Nothing Come from? Commentary on Wilson

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

180 North Michigan Avenue, #2220
Chicago, IL 60601
E-mail: belitowitz@aol.com

Bonnie Litowitz   Faculty, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis; Associate Professor, Department of Psychiatry, Rush Medical School.

Mitchell Wilson's paper is motivated by his observation that the clinical literature is filled with examples of the salutary effects of missing the patient (in various ways). Descriptions of enactments, misunderstandings, and failures of empathy that spontaneously arise in treatment have all been characterized as moving the analytic process forward. In other words, despite technical exhortations for how and when to interpret (properly timed, tactfully, first at the defenses, etc.), analysts constantly fail and their patient benefits from those failures.

Wilson acknowledges that analysts have tried to explain the positive effects of missing the patient from many different theoretical positions; but, he feels, a more general explanatory theory can be found in the concept of “the lack” from Lacanian theory. Wilson claims that analysts who do not recognize the ubiquity of the lack (and its relation to desire) respond defensively with premature closures that he identifies as obsessional solutions.

Any theory offering itself as a better explanatory solution faces a paradox: how to achieve its aim without falling into the same class as those it finds are in error. Like the barbers and liars of classic paradoxes, Wilson's theoretical explanation must be in a class apart from the others.1 Other theoretical explanations are about something; his is about nothing.

1Solving the paradox requires separating metalanguage from object (referent) language. In natural language these are one and the same, so the distinction depends on the context of use (i.e., the frame). Throughout his paper, with terms such as nothing and lack, Wilson shifts from one level to the other.435

Psychoanalysis occupie…roblematic position in regard to international diplomacy and world conflict. It once was assumed that familiarity with the unconscious and the destructive tendencies inherent in human nature might offer analyst…nique and privileged position from which to understand and attempt to contribute to the resolution of national and international crises. In the aftermath of World War I, for example, Freud (1932) was asked to comment on whether human nature made war inevitable.

For the most part, however, assumptions about the relevance and applicability of psychoanalysis to diplomacy and world affairs have not been borne out by experience. Despite the pioneering work on small-group dynamics by Freud, Bion, and others, the explanatory power of analytic theories and the clinical data on which analytic expertise is based have proven more relevant to understanding individual and dyadic behavior and emotional development than to understanding experience and behavior in large social groups.

273

responseSet.title: Response to Commentaries
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Commentaries

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson  

It is a privilege to have my work examined so closely and thoughtfully by respected colleagues. I am very appreciative of their efforts. I will not, of course, be able to respond to every point made or observation offered. I intend my remarks to clarify my point of view, to address what I think are misunderstandings of my point of view, and to discuss further a few of the more vexing aspects of my paper.

I have aimed to highlight the crucial importance of lack in the generation of meaning within the analytic field and in the analyst's basic clinical position. I can see where it might be hard for a reader or commentator to find a place to speak within a broad landscape whose outer boundaries are clearly marked (“the nothing that is the source of fear and the nothing that is the source of help”) and within which an essential dialectic (“meaning's presence and the absences that necessarily haunt it”) is described and elaborated in detail. As best I can tell, in order to find such a place both Reed and Litowitz represent my views in their most extreme form: they claim that I am blindly championing “nothing,” and that I suggest the analyst can or should do “nothing.” Reed says I make a “pathology” of the analyst's attempts at understanding. I would say that their obscuring of one side of the dialectic allows them then to take up the side they've bracketed: this is the cause of meaning, the importance of evolving truths within the analytic setting, the insistence that the analyst must do “something.” And who could possibly argue with that? But of course the main point I make is that the positive giving of meaning is itself necessarily lacking, and that this lacking is an important part of its value. Why? Because an intervention can lead to something more, hopefully something new, only if it lacks something in its content or the form of its expression.1

Perspectives on Clinical Theory

Gender as Soft Assembly (Relational Perspectives Book Series, Vol. 25). By Adrienne Harris. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2005, 336 pp., $57.50.

Barbara Stimmel

Barbara Stimmel

1185 Park Avenue
New York, NY 10128

Barbara Stimmel   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Freudian Society; Assistant Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, Mount Sinai School of Medicine.

Adrienne Harris is a serious advocate for a very particular, and currently popular, perspective on psychoanalytic theory and process. The working assumption that psychoanalysis needs a relational corrective, particularly in its developmental models, is the theoretical spine of her book, volume 25 in the Relational Perspectives Series. Relational psychoanalysts protest “the theorist … as authority, the magisterial one who knows” (p. 9) and in so doing have developed ways of speaking and thinking that have become almost impossible to challenge without mistakenly being considered outdated, even out of touch. Harris's words make most clear what she hopes to accomplish; the following passage may serve as the backdrop of this review, in which I hope to continue an important debate about serious differences which, even when enriching our theoretical matrix, sometimes threaten its foundation:

The developmental models I use—variations of chaos theory and complexity theory—all privilege variation over the statistical concept of central tendency, a focus on the average or mean values of any behavior or experience. Because I am interested in variation within individuals as well as between people, I use the term soft assembly to describe the protean, multipathed but planful and patterned unfolding of experience, such as becoming gendered. Multiplicity offers a certain psychic flexibility that, in relation to shifting objective and social contexts, is distinctly adaptive [pp. 28-29].

Harris repeatedly reminds the reader that all of mental life is “variable,” “multilevel,” “multiple,” “manifold,” “multifunctional,” “multidimensional,” and more.

Anita Schmukler   Training and Supervising Analyst and Child Supervisor, Philadelphia Center for Psychoanalysis; faculty and Child Supervisor, NYU Psychoanalytic Institute.

One of the ways that the Psychoanalytic Institute of New York University chose to celebrate its fiftieth anniversary was to hold an all-day symposium of two panels. The first of these, focusing on how theoretical perspectives inform clinical data, is the subject of this fine volume. Following the event, the panelists and presenter reconvened,

343

providing them the opportunity to reflect on the proceedings, reconsider viewpoints at variance from their own, and in the process integrate new material into their thinking. The result is a veritable raceme of material provided by presenter, panelists, audience, and thoughtful editors. Discerning editorial commentaries precede the presentation of clinical material (my only concern is that the point of view of some of the summaries might skew the impressions of inexperienced readers). There is also a thoughtful preface and introduction, and an appendix in which questions raised by the panelists are further considered.

The central focus of this volume is Claudia Lament's sensitive and detailed presentation of three consecutive sessions from work with a patient who at the time had been in analysis for two years and nine months. Lament was refreshingly open in expressing her concerns, frustrations, thinking about potential interventions, and personal history. The reader is thus made witness to a profound movement, development, and maturation in both patient and analyst.

For Lament, a significant obstacle in her use of defense analysis with this thirty-year-old male patient was his primitive nature, expressed in part by his terror of his childhood, and thus the reexperience of it, and his lack of basic trust, which he concealed “beneath an illusory wall of oneness and merger with the other” (p. 71). Thus, while the analyst tried to attend to conflicts and defenses, her patient was despairing of the use of language itself to communicate, and unable to trust in an elemental manner while struggling to hold on to an ambivalent relationship. One particular juncture of treatment was of great interest to all. When Lament directly confronted the obstacles to progress, examining her own despair, she engaged in a reverie following a most difficult session in which she associated to a child patient treated previously and to her own early experiences. She struggled with how the patient made words meaningless. From this vantage point, she was better able to serve as a witness to her patient in his painfully despondent state. She was then able to interpret his feelings in a manner that addressed his current needs. Lament's sensitivity in formulating her interpretation brought to my mind a paper by Andrew Peto (1960), who examined the hazards of transference interpretations for patients with primitive pathology who are not yet ready for the potential disruptions that can ensue.

Freud's Requiem: Mourning, Memory, and the Invisible History of a Summer's Walk. By Matthew von Unwerth. New York: Riverhead, 2005, 244 pp., $23.95.

Stanley Leavy

Stanley Leavy

200 Leeder Hill Drive
Hamden, CT 06517
E-mail: tanleavy@aol.com

Stanley Leavy   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale University (retired); Training and Supervising Analyst, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis (retired).

Freud was a late representative of the Enlightenment in his devotion to the scientific study of human life and mind—in his secularism, his classicism, and his humanism. From another angle, the debt of psychoanalysis to romanticism is also visible, with its emphasis on the expression of the emotions, and on the hidden depths of motivation. Perhaps we can best locate its cultural background in a convergence of the two. Matthew von Unwerth's graciously written book, Freud's Requiem, is as good a guide to this juncture as one could ask for.

Taking as its point of departure Freud's short essay of the year 1915, “On Transience” (which, happily, is reprinted in the volume), and returning to it as a repeated point of reference in many contexts, von Unwerth gives us a mini-biography of each of the three people memorialized in the famous “summer walk in a smiling countryside” with which the essay begins: Freud, Rainer Maria Rilke, and Lou Andreas-Salomé. The latter two are presented anonymously in the essay, and von Unwerth sympathizes with the opinion that it is a fiction based on less idyllic meetings, but no matter: all three are shown true to life.

In Freud's brief narrative of the walk, the poet Rilke laments the passage of all that is beautiful in nature and art. While Lou Andreas-Salomé, the lover of Rilke at an earlier period of his life, stays silent, Freud protests, to the contrary, that it is transience that underlies the allure of the world of life and nature and art. The flowering summer, so soon to depart, the works of art, that will ultimately disappear, life itself in its brevity, these engage our affections on that very account—that we cannot hold on to them. It is failing to accept loss that deprives the poet of appreciation of the present beauty. What in the lives of these three very different persons has brought about both their meeting and the many threads of experience woven into the “take” of each on these questions about ultimate meaning?

Von Unwerth replies to that question with a method akin to the metaphor of the “mycelium” (the mass of threads forming the growing structure of a fungus) of ideas that Freud employed in accounting for the formation of dreams.

Psychotherapists in the Community: Reported Prototypical Psychodynamic Treatments of Trauma

Michele Schottenbauer , Diane Arnkoff , Carol Glass and Sheila Gray

Sheila Gray

314 O'Boyle Hall
DC 20064
E-mail: maschotten@aol.com

Sheila Gray  

The effort to categorize psychotherapeutic treatments according to their efficacy has in the past decade led to a number of lists of empirically supported treatments (ESTs; Chambless and Ollendick 2001). With regard to trauma, the primary treatments that have undergone the rigorous empirical testing necessary to be included in lists of ESTs (e.g., Nathan and Gorman 1998; Roth and Fonagy 2005) are largely cognitive—behavioral treatments and eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR; Shapiro 1995). Nevertheless, there are many indications that clinicians in the community use psychodynamic psychotherapy for treating trauma. A recent guideline for psychiatrists on the treatment of PTSD notes clinical consensus on the usefulness of psychodynamic psychotherapy in treating certain types of trauma, particularly in cases where interpersonal functioning is substantially impacted (APA 2004). Empirical research reveals that many clinicians in the community employ psychodynamic interventions for trauma (Rodriguez et al. 2003). While a number of authors have described psychodynamic approaches with patients who have experienced trauma (Horowitz 1997; Krupnick 2002; Lindy 1993), little is known about the nature of psychodynamic psychotherapy for trauma as actually conducted by clinicians in the community.

This short and engaging book contains a series of papers that were originally presented in Tokyo at the Third International Margaret S. Mahler Symposium on Child Development held April 1-2, 2000. While the symposium and the book were dedicated to the work and memory of Margaret Mahler, the subject at hand is affect theory. There is no better way to suggest the spirit of this book than to quote Salman Akhtar's story about Buddha:

A man approached Buddha while he was sitting, eyes closed, under a Banyan tree meditating. Amid sobs and tears, the man reported that his son was very ill and the local healers had given up on the child. The boy was about to die. The man pleaded for divine intervention from Buddha. He cried, wailed, touched Buddha's feet. Buddha, however, sat motionless and neither opened his eyes nor said anything in response. The man left, only to appear the next day filled with rage His son had died and he held Buddha's inactivity responsible for it. He shouted obscenities, cursed Buddha, and still finding no visible response, spat at him in disgust and left.

Time passed and a day came, a few years later, when the man returned to visit Buddha again. Now he was very remorseful. He said that, over time, he had gradually realized that by remaining silent, Buddha was conveying to him two important messages, that there is little he could do in a situation if those who knew about physical ailments had given up, and that there are no words to offer solace to a man whose son is about to die. The man was guilt-ridden for his having spat on Buddha. Crying and holding Buddha's feet, he begged for forgiveness. It was then that Buddha opened his eyes and spoke. He said “You spat on a river and the water floated away. The man I was then is gone with time. I am different. You did not spit on me and hence I have no authority to forgive you. But it makes me sad that while you have learnt many things, you are still standing on the same spot on the riverbank. You are being consumed by a moment that has long departed. It is not I, but you, and only you, who can release yourself from this bondage” [pp. 118-119].

Cecilia Samish  

Isabel Paret kicked off the panel after a brief introduction by Arthur J. Farley. Much of the material she presented is taken from the book Complex Adoption and Assisted Reproductive Technology: A Developmental Approach to Clinical Practice (Shapiro, Shapiro, and Paret 2001).

Assisted Reproductive Technology, hereafter referred to as ART, is used by infertile heterosexual couples, gays and lesbians, and single parents by choice, thus helping to change the cultural meaning of family. That each person has the right to experience parenthood is increasingly acknowledged in our society; that, in turn, has led to greater acceptance and respect for nontraditional families. Social acceptance or its lack can be very important in the life of children and their parents.

Forming a family through ART requires long-term effort, psychological stamina, and financial resources. It involves physical risk and emotional pain.

Paret gave a brief description of various forms of ART. It is possible for a child to have five different parental figures: the egg donor, the sperm donor, the gestastional mother, and two intentional parents.

Intentional parents face unique developmental tasks, beginning with facing the grief of infertility. Psychoanalysts can help with this process, but for many years they stressed the psychological basis of infertility. A study group in the Boston Psychoanalytic Society published fascinating material in 2002 on how analysis can help people undergoing medical treatments in order to create a pregnancy.

In vitro fertilization (IVF)

Fred Busch   Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of New England, East; faculty, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute and Massachusetts Institute of Psychoanalysis.

In this book Stefano Bolognini, a training and supervising analyst of the Italian Psychoanalytic Society, has written a wise, thoughtful, and elegantly clear exploration of one of our basic yet still mysterious concepts: empathy. His chosen path in the book is the exploration of empathy rather than its emphasis. His spare prose succinctly outlines his views in a way that boils down complex arguments to their essentials. I often found myself looking over what I had just read with the question, “Did he really just explain that in three pages?”

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Fear of Trying: Dysfunctional Conservatism

Newell Fischer

Newell Fischer

658 Black Rock Road
Bryn Mawr, PA 19010
Fax: 610-581-0510
E-mail: fischer2@mail.med.upenn.edu

Newell Fischer   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, School of Medicine, University of Pennsylvania; Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Center of Philadelphia.

As the American Psychoanalytic Association faces mounting external pressures that threaten its very survival, its leadership and members appear increasingly preoccupied with issues of internal governance and training standards. This inward gazing, though in keeping with analysts' personality and occupational traits, serves to obscure and defend against the anxieties associated with threats from the surround. Such maladaptive defensive activity contributes to organizational stagnation, dysfunctional conservatism, and a “fear of trying.” Suggestions to combat this self-defeating behavior are offered.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Two to Tango: Commentary on Celenza

Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

80 University Place 5th floor
New York, NY 10003
Fax: 212-226-4152
E-mail: aharris@psychoanalysis.net

Adrienne Harris   Faculty and Supervisor, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

I want to begin by expressing my admiration for Andrea Celenza's particular strategy in developing her theoretical argument, as well as for the subtlety and courage in her clinical work and its report. What is so useful and necessary to me in Celenza's approach is that she sets up a complex matrix of experience as she introduces us to the particular locus of trouble, the gendered problematics of desire and aggression in a heterosexual analytic couple in which the analyst is a woman. She asks that we think about the wider culture, the narrower, historically specific psychoanalytic culture we are operating within, the stereotypes of gender we are embedded in, and the infusion and confusion of cultural stereotype and gender ideology into psychoanalytic theory and practice. Gender arrangements intersect with the structure, and muscle, and bones of transference in ways that are sometimes surprising and sometimes obvious.

Right from the outset of this demanding and fascinating clinical case, Celenza immerses her readers in complex grids of power and constriction, domination and dependency. Drawing on literature over the past two decades, Celenza notes how unremarkably the transference to a woman analyst has been assumed to be maternal, regressive, and pre-oedipal. Celenza traces the historical shifts in modes of clinical work from a constricted and emotionally narrow-gauge style to more broad-spectrum, deep engagements with patients. The widening relational perspective (along with other cultural shifts as well) may have made the crucible of transference and countertransference more volatile and more fully susceptible to expression and enactment. The frame and setup of analysis has the power to set off powerful fantasies of domination and intimacy. As Celenza reminds us, these fantasies often come in their own unique, quite luridly gendered colors.

Assessing the Quality of Randomized Controlled Trials of Psychodynamic Psychotherapy

Andrew Gerber , James Kocsis , Barbara Milrod and Steven Roose

Steven Roose

1051 Riverside Drive Unit 74
New York, NY 10032
E-mail: agerber@aya.yale.edu

Steven Roose  

In recent years there have been increasing calls for quantitative empirical research supporting the efficacy and effectiveness of psychoanalytic treatments (Fonagy et al. 2001; Gabbard, Gunderson, and Fonagy 2002; Leuzinger-Bohleber and Target 2002; Wallerstein 2003). Contemporary standards of evidence-based medical treatments and scientific verifiability require that psychoanalytic psychotherapy and psychoanalysis submit to standardized systematic evaluation if they are to continue their claims to be valid and effective treatments. The empirically supported psychotherapy movement, though controversial, has advanced in the last ten years, and there is growing agreement that all psychotherapies must use these standards for evaluation (Bergin and Garfield 1994; Chambless et al. 1996; Norcross 2001). Organizations funding psychotherapy, including insurance companies, government agencies, and employers, have been especially vocal in demanding empirical evidence for the value of a treatment before devoting resources to it. Agencies funding research, including the National Institutes of Health in the United States and analogous agencies in Europe and South America, have taken up the call as well; they fund psychotherapy research on the condition that it is part of this central move toward an empirically supported approach. Finally, organizations responsible for the education of mental health professionals (e.g., the American Association of Directors of Psychatric Residency Training), are debating whether, given limited resources and time, programs must focus their training on psychotherapies meeting evidence-based criteria.

Consistent with its hundred-year history of conflicted relations with empirical research, psychoanalysis has reacted to these increasing pressures in complicated ways (Blatt 2001; Luborsky 2001

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Teaching Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy: Voices that have Reach

Fred Griffin

Fred Griffin

CPM 433
1530 3rd Avenue South
Birmingham, AL 35294-0018
E-mail: flgriffin@uabmc.edu

Fred Griffin   Training and Supervising Analyst, New Orleans-Birmingham Psychoanalytic Center; Associate Professor, Department of Psychiatry, University of Alabama School of Medicine.

Psychoanalytic psychotherapy is countercultural. In graduate schools and psychiatric residencies in the twenty-first century, students are faced with a menu of “the psychotherapies” and are often dissuaded from developing skills in a kind of psychotherapy that requires time for its unfolding and immersion in a relationship with another human being. Students are often encouraged to learn approaches that are “evidence-based,” that may be more singularly behavioral or psychopharmacological, and that are “more practical.” Many are skeptical about the effectiveness of psychoanalytic psychotherapy and doubtful that it has a future. This is an educational culture quite different from the one in which many of us from an earlier generation chose our professional paths.

To provide a good beginning, teachers and supervisors must introduce both orienting principles and ways for the student of psychotherapy to imagine what it is like to do psychotherapy—at a time before they have had convincing clinical experience or the benefits of a personal therapy or analysis. A psychotherapy text may generate a similar educational experience for the reader by presenting psychoanalytic concepts incisively, by providing accessible metaphors that illuminate the psychotherapeutic process, and by demonstrating the author's authentic work with patients.

A book on psychoanalytic psychotherapy that sets out to teach today's students must be engaging, persuasive, and alive. The voice of its author must be believable—one that has “the whole weight of life … behind it … that will stretch out to the reader, make him prick up his ears and attend” (Alvarez 2005, pp. 9, 16). It is a voice that must have reach.

In her essay “Finding a Voice,” Eudora Welty (1983) provides a metaphor for the ways that the more experienced teacher may assist the novice in finding her own way.

Lila Kalinich

Lila Kalinich

333 Central Park West, Apt. 12
New York, NY 10025

Lila Kalinich   Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

In Act 4, scene 1, of Shakespeare's Richard II, Richard the eponymous king abdicates his throne to his cousin and rival Harry Bolingbroke. Earlier in the play, in an example of the caprice and malfeasance that apparently typified Richard's reign, the king had seized the property and financial assets of Harry, banished earlier, to fund a war against the Irish that England could ill afford. This strategic failure, having left Richard unprepared to defend his kingdom against his rival's unanticipated return, resulted in the loss of his throne. Though the abdication is portrayed as voluntary, Shakespeare makes clear that Richard had no choice. In a moving speech, Richard unravels before the audience:

For I have given here my soul's consent

T'undeck the pompous body of a king,

Made glory base and sovereignty a slave,

Proud majesty a subject, state a peasant.

Aims and Outcomes of Psychoanalysis

Rosemary Cogan

Rosemary Cogan

TX 79409-2051
E-mail: r.cogan@ttu.edu

Rosemary Cogan  

Learning about the ideal outcome of psychoanalysis has been viewed as the optimum strategy for understanding positive mental health (Jahoda 1955). Here psychoanalysts' descriptions of their ideal of patients at the end of psychoanalysis are compared with psychoanalysts? descriptions of real patients at the end of psychoanalysis with terminations mutually agreed on by analysts and analysands, with and without maximum benefit, as a way of understanding more about the goals of psychoanalysis and analysts? views of positive mental health.

[[RunningHead]]

Do Psychiatry Residents Obtain Informed Consent for Psychotherapy?

Bret Rutherford and Steven Roose

Steven Roose

1051 Riverside Drive
Box 98
New York, NY 10032, MA, 01266
E-mail: brr8@columbia.edu

Steven Roose  

This study investigated how psychiatry residents conduct the opening phase of treatment with different kinds of patients. Particular attention was paid to whether they obtain informed consent for treatment and whether patient diagnosis or treatment modality affects informed consent discussions.

Background

In psychiatry, as in other fields of medicine, informed consent was first applied to arguably invasive procedures such as electroconvulsive therapy. Only subsequently has its purview been broadened, first to psychopharmacological treatment and, more recently, to psychotherapy (Beahrs and Gutheil 2001). Despite explicit mention in the codes of ethics adopted by the American Psychiatric Association, the American Psychological Association, and the American Psychoanalytic Association, informed consent for psychotherapy is seen as problematic by many clinicians. Recent studies have identified variable attention to informed consent, with psychodynamically oriented clinicians obtaining it for psychotherapy less often than other mental health professionals (Croarkin, Berg, and Spira 2003). Psychoanalysts and psychodynamic psychotherapists may feel that the disclosure entailed by a robust informed consent discussion violates analyst anonymity or disturbs the transference and the psychoanalytic process (Robitscher 1978). Psychiatry residents, given their still incomplete knowledge and uncertain sense of professional authority, may have more difficulty than practicing psychiatrists in obtaining informed consent from their patients.

Stephen Rosenblum

Stephen Rosenblum

2405 I Street, N.W., Apt. 3B
Washington, DC 20037
Fax 301-299-0282
E-mail: gsrosenblm@aol.com

Stephen Rosenblum   Training and Supervising Analyst, Washington Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, George Washington University Medical School.

The Dynamics of Human Aggression is the culmination of over thirty years of collaborative and independent work by its authors. Although the book is clearly a joint effort, significant components of the theoretical framework have been published over the past twenty-five years in books and papers by Meissner (there are thirty-six references to his work alone in the bibliography). In this compact volume, the authors propose a revolutionary revision in the psychoanalytic theory of motivation and aggression. They start with the conviction that drive theory, by removing agency and responsibility for actions from the person and ascribing it to an impersonal force pressing for discharge, fails to accurately explain human activity.

Psychoanalysis and Medicine in the Time of Freud and Brill: Commentary on Richards

Sander Gilman

Sander Gilman

S420 Callaway Center
Atlanta GA 30322-0660
E-mail: Sander34@aol.com

Sander Gilman   Distinguished Professor of Liberal Arts and Sciences, Emory University.

Arnold Richards has presented a compelling case for the “Americanization” of psychoanalysis by A.[braham] A.[rden] Brill, an Eastern European Jew desiring to become an American. He is quite candid about how this process impacted on the history of psychoanalytic theory and practice in the United States (See Heinze 2004, pp. 188-121; Hale 1995; Schwartz 1999). The overarching power of psychoanalysis to shape American self-consciousness in the twentieth century brought about a transformation analogous to the creation of the image of America in cinema by the Eastern European Jews who shaped the movie business at the same moment (see Gabler 1989). Richards is quite correct in sketching the importance of the difference between Sigmund Freud and A. A. Brill as individuals in the creation of psychoanalysis and its American reception-especially in terms of the centrality of their understanding of education and culture (Bildung) to their personal sense of integration into a “Christian” world (Catholic in Vienna; Protestant in New York).

Eastern European Jews were being integrated into such a world in Vienna by the middle of the nineteenth century. Vienna became politically anti-Semitic decades later. In New York City it was only at the beginning of the twentieth century that Eastern European Jews began to enter the world of education and social mobility. Many of these Eastern European Jews in Vienna, as well as in New York, came from the eastern areas of the Austro-Hungarian Empire and from Imperial Russia. In Austria, by midcentury, these Ostjuden had slowly begun to enter the world of the professions (see Gilman 1993a, b).

379

[[RunningHead]]

The Reader's Lack: Commentary on Wilson

Gail Reed

Gail Reed

1199 Park Avenue #1—G
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: gail.reed@aya.yale.edu

Gail Reed   Training and Supervising Analyst, Berkshire Psychoanalytic Institute and the Training Institute of the New York Freudian Society; faculty, Cleveland Psychoanalytic Center.

In his article, Mitchell Wilson reaches outside the usual North American references to less well-known European ideas. Besides his use of Lacanian theory, his writing about lack appears to resonate with André, Green's idea of a void, a concept that can profoundly change one's clinical perspective (Reed 2001, 2003; Reed and Baudry 2005). Moreover, he happily advocates an analytic attitude in which the analyst is aware of his or her limitations and his or her contribution to the analytic process, and in which the analyst is cognizant of the way theory can easily become belief, and exploration of an individual's unconscious an exercise in verification of that belief. I tend to agree with these clinical propositions and applaud Wilson's reaching beyond our familiar references. It was therefore a surprise, as I continued to read his article, to find myself becoming quite uncomfortable with it. I was puzzled by my reaction and at a loss about what to say.

It seemed, on reflection, that Wilson's text had an additional register, beyond its surface intent, and that this more silent register was causing me considerable discomfort. Indeed, my loss about what to say suggested to me that Wilson's text was evoking in me a sense of my own lack. I began to wonder whether these feelings could be related to Wilson's work with Evan. After all, parallelism phenomena are not limited to the supervisory relationship (Arlow 1960), but have been shown to occur in the relationship set up between a reader and a literary text (Reed 1982), so why not in a paper that attempts to use theory to work out a countertransference?

The author thanks Francis Baudry for helpful comments.

447

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What are we Doing and Why?

Robert Glick

Robert Glick

E-mail: rag4@columbia.edu

Robert Glick  

In this Education Section, we continue our serious self-scrutiny of psychoanalytic education. With the papers in this issue, the necessary, if uncomfortable, examination of our best intentions and efforts focuses on current expressions of the “treat/teach” dilemma. Hence my title: “What are we doing and why”

Supervision (more about supervision in subsequent sections) stands closest to the ancient professional guild apprenticeship tradition, a tradition that has seen clinical practice as the predominant goal, and therefore views the other two legs of our tripartite structure—training analysis and didactic education—as in many ways necessary and justified, but as untouchable in the first instance and secondary in the latter. The training analysis and the didactic curriculum are the subjects of this section. While differing in their approaches, the two papers demand that we consider what we aim to achieve as psychoanalytic educators and how we know if we are succeeding.

Training analysis, “the cornerstone of psychoanalytic training,” has long been considered sacrosanct and almost mythical in its educational/ therapeutic purposes, essential for becoming an analyst but untested as to its effectiveness in achieving its aims. Beginning with a revisiting of Freud's original prescription of a personal analysis in his apprenticeship vision of psychoanalytic education, Cabaniss and Bosworth, in “The Aim of the Training Analysis,” take us through the history of our field's thinking on the goals of the training analysis: the tensions, ideals, and issues that have occupied our educators for nearly a century. Their paper is a sober, humbling, and, at times almost amusing reminder of where we have been-our idealism and grand expectations-and where we need to go. The authors end with a critique and an appeal for a systematic study of the training analysis so that we can clarify how our aims can reciprocally affect our technique, and ultimately develop methods of assessing how effectively a training analysis achieves its aims.

[[RunningHead]]

Making Death Thinkable: A Psychoanalytic Contribution to the Problem of the Transience of Life. By Franco De Masi. London: Free Association Books, 2004, 157 pp., $34.50.

Stanley Leavy

Stanley Leavy

200 Leeder Hill Drive
Hamden, CT 06517
E-mail: stanleavy@aol.com

Stanley Leavy   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale University (retired); Training and Supervising Analyst, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis (retired).

Psychoanalytic treatments can proceed for many years without more than passing notice being granted to the inevitable reality of one's own death. Especially in the first half of life, which is the period in which most analyses are undertaken, many concerns seem more pressing than the termination of life. Death can be put off as a prospect to the remote future, and anyway is not alterable by what we think about it. But the curious effect of evading consideration of death is that analyst and analysand are omitting—or censoring—from the orbit of associations what is literally a defining thought about human existence. Although all living things, and especially all animals, act to defend or prolong their lives, only humans, as far as we know, are aware of their mortality. That ought to make a difference in the way we lead our lives, and in the way we think about our lives during psychoanalysis. To avoid the idea of death is to be inauthentic about life.

Making Death Thinkable is a compilation of short papers approaching its topic from several angles. While this makes for a degree of disconnection between the chapters, and some repetition, it has the corresponding benefit of promoting an open-minded attitude to a variety of theoretical models. Franco De Masi never strikes one as stuck in a single inflexible mode; this, taken with a comfortable informality of style, put into fluent English by the translator,1 disarms that resistance to the subject which is one of the themes of the book.

1Some not easily recognizable transliterations are at first puzzling—e.g., “Cajkovskij” for the Russian composer, usually rendered as “Tchaikovski” (or something similar) and “Fedon” for the protagonist of Plato's Phaedo. A less trivial license of another sort occurs on page 136, where Shakespeare's character Cassius (Julius Caesar, I.ii.139) is given a statement about death that quite distorts the original, which has to do with insurrection, not death.304

Caroline de Pottél  

An Analog Study of Envy

The aim of this study is to view countertransference responses to patients presenting with envy through an empirical lens within a psychoanalytic framework. Envy is a complicated and challenging emotion considered ubiquitous in human experience. However, no research has been conducted to understand psychotherapist/analyst identification and clinical judgments of envy. Envy literature is reviewed from psychoanalytic, social psychology, and empirical research perspectives. A negative view of patients presenting with envy is implied through the use of cases with severe levels of psychopathology (see, e.g., Klein 1957; Riviere 1935; Kernberg 1984; Lopez-Corvo 1995). The categories of situational (State) envy and trait (Schema) envy are identified and used in the study (Spillius 1993; Montaldi 1999).

Methods

The analog method of research is used to combine the richness of the psychoanalytic case study and the rigor of empirical methods. Eighty-four practicing clinical psychologists, half of them male and half female, responded to five written vignettes depicting psychotherapy sessions illustrating the themes of Impulsivity, Dependency, Shame, Schema Envy, and State Envy for identification. Measures used as indicators of countertransference include the Clinical Themes Rating Scale (CTRS)

What's in a Name: What Analyst and Patient Call Each other

Grace Barron

Grace Barron

350 Central Park West, Suite 1A
New York, NY 10025
E-mail: gcbarron@msn.com

Grace Barron   Assistant Professor of Clinical Psychiatry, Columbia College of Physicians and Surgeons; faculty, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Awkward moments often arise between patient and analyst involving the question, “What do we call each other?” The manner in which the dyad address each other contains material central to the patient's inner life. Names, like dreams, deserve a privileged status as providing a royal road into the paradoxical analytic relationship and the unconscious conflicts that feed it. Whether an analyst addresses the patient formally, informally, or not at all, awareness of the issues surrounding names is important.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Art

Fantastic Reality: Louise Bourgeois and a Story of Modern Art. By Mignon Nixon. Cambridge: MIT Press, 2005, xi + 338 pp., 103 illustrations, $40.00.

Laura Meyer

Laura Meyer

5225 North Backer Avenue M/S CA65
California State University
Fresno, CA 93740-8001
Fax: 559-278-4706
E-mail: lmeyer@csufresno.edu

Laura Meyer   Assistant Professor of Art History, California State University, Fresno

Nonagenarian sculptor Louise Bourgeois has in recent years emerged as one of the most highly visible and popular figures on the international art scene. Recognition, however, has come neither easily nor early to the artist. Born in 1911, Bourgeois studied at various Paris ateliers in the 1930s, when the surrealists held sway over the European avant-garde. After marrying the American art historian Robert Goldwater in 1938 and relocating to New York City, she continued in the orbit of the surrealists, many of whom sought refuge in the United States during the Second World War. She achieved modest success in the 1940s, showing paintings, prints, and sculptures in venues associated with the surrealists and the emerging New York School. In contrast to her better-known male peers, however, Bourgeois languished in relative obscurity throughout the 1950s and 1960s, and waited for her first major museum exhibition (at New York's Museum of Modern Art) until 1982, when she was seventy years old. Since that time, she has been the focus of hundreds of exhibitions, illustrated catalogues, and other honors, including representing the United States at the 1993 Venice Biennale and inaugurating London's new Tate Gallery of Modern Art in 2000 with its first one-person exhibition. Even so, serious scholarly attention to Bourgeois's career has continued to lag behind popular interest in the artist and her work.

Mignon Nixon's ambitious and illuminating analysis, Fantastic Reality: Louise Bourgeois and a Story of Modern Art, is the first book-length scholarly monograph devoted to the artist and a highly welcome addition to the field of cultural studies. Nixon finds an apt approach to Bourgeois's art through psychoanalysis, a discourse that, like modern art, spans the twentieth century.

1015

Marie Rudden , Barbara Milrod , Mary Target , Steven Ackerman and Elizabeth Graf

Elizabeth Graf

Elizabeth Graf  

This pilot process study is part of a larger randomized controlled trial of Panic—Focused Psychodynamic Psychotherapy (PFPP) comparing it with a placebo—control treatment, Applied Relaxation Therapy (ART). Of 49 subjects in the total RCT sample, 26 were in PFPP and 29 in ART. Treatment outcome was evaluated at termination by raters blinded to patient condition and therapist orientation, and at 2, 4, 6, and 12 months post—treatment, using diagnostic and symptom measures recommended by the NIMH consensus conference on treatment of panic disorder (Milrod et al. in press).

Kenneth Levy , Rachel Wasserman , Lori Scott , Sanno Zach , Candace White , Nicole Cain , John Clarkin and Otto Kernberg

Borderline personality disorder (BPD) is a prevalent, chronic, and serious psychiatric problem characterized by a pattern of chaotic and self-defeating interpersonal relationships, emotional lability, identity disturbance, poor impulse control, frequent angry outbursts, frequent suicidality, and self-mutilation (Skodol et al. 2002).

Psychotherapy is the recommended primary technique for treating BPD patients (Oldham et al. 2001) and is the most widely practiced approach to their treatment. A meta-analysis (Perry, Banon, and Ianni 1999)

[[RunningHead]]

Mentalization, Affect Regulation, and Development of the Self

Elizabeth Weinberg

Elizabeth Weinberg

5300 San Jacinto, Suite 190
Houston, TX 77004
Fax: 713-522-1858
E-mail: eweinberg@swbell.net

Elizabeth Weinberg  

The panel chair, Glen Gabbard, opened the two-day panel with some reflections on the place of mentalization theory in psychoanalytic discourse. Although in recent years we have had few new paradigms, mentalization theory appears to be a genuinely new model of psychoanalytic developmental and clinical theory. This model extends the clinical observations of object relations theory and calls to mind Fairbairn's assertion that the libidinal motivation of the infant is primarily “object seeking.” Mentalization theory provides models both of early childhood psychological development and of psychotherapeutic practice. The fundamental therapeutic application of mentalization to psychodynamic technique lies in the observation that adult patients may experience corrective developmental experiences “when the patient finds himself in the eyes of the therapist.”

Day I: Psychopathology

Peter Fonagy gave a detailed presentation based on work he and his colleagues Target, Gergely, and Juris have done. In this work, mentalization is defined as the “implicit or explicit perception or interpretation of the actions of others and oneself as intentional.” This process occurs unconsciously and naturally, and is distinguished from conscious self-reflection. Mentalization theory is similar to the concept of “theory of mind” in the cognitive developmental literature, but is more comprehensive in that it includes attachment and the development of the self.

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, San Francisco, June 26-27, 2004.

Panelists: Glen Gabbard (chair), Peter Fonagy, Gyorgy Gergely, Mary Target. Discussants: Robert Michels, Owen Renik.

responseSet.title: Masculinities, Plural
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Masculinities, Plural

Ethel Person

Ethel Person

135 Central Park West
# 11 South
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: espersonany@aol.com

Ethel Person   Professor of Clinical Psychiatry, College of Physicians and Surgeons, Columbia University; Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Masculinity cannot be regarded as a single entity. Both within Western culture and across cultures, a wide variety of masculinities are easily observable. Yet masculinity is so often contrasted with femininity that the many differences among men are at times obscured. To in part correct this deficiency, various “psychologies” of men are explored, as well as the cultural components that shape a society's ideas of what constitutes masculinity. Male heterosexuality and homosexuality are also examined, as are a number of the fantasies and fears that men typically experience. What cannot be left out of any exploration of male psychology are those sources of strength that permit so many men to fiercely protect their families and, when called upon, to fight their country's wars. Nonetheless, the differences between individual men are significant and can even be said to be vast.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Minding the Gap Between Positivism and Hermeneutics in Psychoanalytic Research

Patrick Luyten , Sidney Blatt and Jozef Corveleyn

Jozef Corveleyn

Tiensestraat 102
3000 Leuven
Belgium
Fax: ++32-16/32 59 20
E-mail: patrick.luyten@psy.kuleuven.ac.be

Jozef Corveleyn   Professor of Psychology, Department of Psychology, Faculty of Psychology and Educational Sciences, Catholic University of Leuven (Belgium).

Two quite different cultures are to be found within psychoanalysis, one more clinical in orientation, more focused on meaning and interpretation, and relying primarily on the traditional case study method, the other more research-oriented, focused on cause-and-effect relationships, and relying primarily on methods borrowed from the natural and social sciences. The history of this divide is reviewed and arguments, pro and con, about the potential contributions of specific types of empirical investigation are discussed. Increasingly, it seems, criticisms concerning the scientific status of psychoanalysis are being responded to by empirical research. This has contributed to a growing recognition within the scientific community of the credibility of aspects of psychoanalytic theories and of the effectiveness of psychodynamic treatment. However, some segments of the psychoanalytic community are concerned that this increase in the quantity and quality of empirical research on psychoanalytic concepts risks creating an empirical one-sidedness, while other segments are concerned that not engaging in systematic empirical research can lead to intellectual isolation, fragmentation, stagnation, and orthodoxy. To counter this polarizing tendency, a recommendation is made for methodological pluralism. Adopting this stance could contribute to an enriched understanding of the clinical process and to the development of new research methodologies to investigate complex psychodynamic hypotheses, thus bridging the gap between the two psychoanalytic cultures, as well as the gap between research and clinical practice.

responseSet.title: Medication as Object
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Medication as Object

Adele Tutter

Adele Tutter

300 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: atutter@mac.com

Adele Tutter   Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Columbia University School of Physicians and Surgeons; Adjunct Clinical Assistant Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College; Candidate, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

People experience and treat medication as though it were a person: in other words, as an object. Among the many symbolic meanings attributed to medication, this sort of personification, or object representation, is a meaning that medication is uniquely positioned to contain and convey: imbued with intentionality and influence, medication moves beyond the sphere of static, iconic representation and enters the changeable, dynamic object world of action, aim, and agency. Unlike more generic or stereotypic meanings, object representations attributed to medication may reflect the patient's specific dynamics and object relations. These representations are many and mutable, and take on shifting and overlapping forms that evolve with the analytic process. Medication may represent a third person within the framework of an analytic treatment, expanding the analytic dyad into a triad and offering new transference paradigms to explore. The defensive displacement of transferential qualities and attitudes, or split-off parts thereof, from the analyst onto medication can serve as a powerful resistance to the awareness of the transference to the analyst. Clinical examples illustrate the utility and importance of the analysis of medication as object, for both patient and analyst, with particular attention to the transference.

Countertransference in Defense Enactments

Fred Busch

Fred Busch

246 Eliot Street
Chestnut Hill, MA 02467
drfredbusch@comcast.net

Fred Busch   Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of New England, East; faculty, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute and Massachusetts Institute of Psychoanalysis.

A clinical term is introduced to capture a defense that develops with the patient's deepening but fleeting awareness of painful transference feelings. The analyst's attention to countertransference in such situations is central to the analysis of these defenses. An attempt is made to distinguish defense enactments from other types of defenses, and to differentiate the analyst's countertransference reaction to this type of defense from countertransference reactions that might appear similar. The reasons for this dynamic in the interpersonal space are explored, and a clinical example that describes this phenomenon in the analytic moment is given.

responseSet.title: Masculinity as Masquerade
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Masculinity as Masquerade

Donald Moss

Donald Moss

80 University Place 5th Floor
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: donaldmoss@mindspring.com

Donald Moss   Faculty, NYU Psychonalytic Institute.

What follows is an improvised reaction to the essays in this issue of JAPA on the theme of masculinity. It is intended as a way both of reflecting back upon, and of continuing, the fascinating, polyphonic exchanges these essays have established.

In the Hollywood summer flick Nacho Libre, the main character, the hilariously unfit Jack Black, assumes a secret identity, affecting the costumed excesses of a caped, macho street wrestler. By day a menial in a monastery, by night he wants to win matches, money, and a woman. In the film's signature moment, Black reassures his protégé, a young boy who has spotted him surreptitiously dressing up, that “it's okay because sometimes a man just goes into his room and puts on stretchy pants and has a lot of fun.” This declaration is meant to mollify the boy's uncomprehending, and suspicious, gaze, to reassure him that his adored older friend, regardless of the stretchy pants, and what used to be the forbidden “feminine” posturing in front of a mirror, remains what he always was: the incarnation of an admirable, straightforward masculinity.

It seems to me that many of us contemporary psychoanalysts occupy a position resembling that of the astonished boy of Nacho Libre. We often can feel ourselves located slightly behind the femininity/masculinity curve, waiting to see what's next, readying ourselves for the necessary adjustments. Over the past few decades, for example, we've been bombarded with well-warranted correctives—from heterosexual feminists, from gays, from lesbians—to what, in retrospect, now seem our outmoded ways of interpreting femininity and masculinity and the bedrock on which they seemed to stand.

We are therefore likely to feel less certain of our old claim to occupy femininity/masculinity's leading theoretical edge. Our position on that edge was bequeathed us by many of Freud's still radical insights.

[[RunningHead]]

“Nothing Could Be Further from the Truth”: The Role of Lack in the Analytic Process

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

2960A Piedmont Avenue
Berkeley, CA 94705
Fax: 510-843-4660
E-mail: mdwmd@pacbell.net

Mitchell Wilson   Faculty, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.

It is an oft-noted clinical phenomenon that the analyst's mistakes are beneficial to the analytic process. Although the analyst's mistakes, misunderstandings, and faulty functioning have been described by psychoanalysts of various theoretical persuasions, no overall theory has been advanced to account for this clinical phenomenon. To address this theoretical lacuna the central Lacanian notions of lack and desire are brought to bear. In particular, lack, or nothing, is presented as an essential working condition of the analyst, one that if understood, recognized, and tolerated can positively inform the analyst's attitude. By contrast, theoretical biases that privilege presence can obscure lack as an important contributor to the analyst's attitude. A clinical case demonstrates that both analyst and patient struggle with deep anxieties generated by lack, and that both are repeatedly tempted to solve these struggles by settling for obsessional solutions.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Aim of the Training Analysis

Deborah Cabaniss and Hillery Bosworth

Hillery Bosworth

903 Park Avenue
New York, NY 10021
e-mail:dsc3@columbia.edu

80 Fifth Avenue #1001
New York, NY 10011
e-mail: hcsb@nyc.rr.com.

Hillery Bosworth   Clinical Assistant Professor of Psychiatry, New York University School of Medicine; Candidate, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Although the training analysis is considered by many to be the most important element of a candidate's analytic training, its aim remains unclear. Some think of the training analysis as being a strictly therapeutic tool, while others assign it a more frankly educational role. This paper reviews the literature on this topic from Freud to the present, discusses the problems that lack of clarity about the aim of the training analysis generates for psychoanalytic education, and proposes future research in this area.

responseSet.title: The Threat of Male-To-Female Erotic Transference
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Threat of Male-To-Female Erotic Transference

Andrea Celenza

Andrea Celenza

11 Field Road
Lexington, MA 02421
E-mail: acelenza@aol.com

Andrea Celenza   Faculty, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Supervising Analyst, Massachusetts Institute of Psychoanalysis; Assistant Clinical Professor, The Cambridge Hospital, Harvard Medical School.

Vignettes from an ongoing psychoanalysis with a patient, Michael, are presented to illustrate the various dimensions of the erotic transference at different phases of the treatment. The relation to power, the experience and expression of aggression, how these may be organized by gender, and the female analyst's countertransference are discussed as potentially fostering or inhibitory in the development of an erotic transference. Traditional sociocultural gender stereotypes kept alive in fantasy can cause female analysts to subtly foreclose the impending threat of an intense erotic transference with male analysands due to a fear of outwardly directed male aggression. It is suggested that the maternal/containing transference can be unconsciously fostered by both analyst and analysand to defensively avoid expression of the aggressivized erotic transference in its full intensity. Similarities and differences in cases of sexual boundary violations with opposite-gender pairings are discussed.

Lewis Kirshner

Lewis Kirshner

306 Harvard Street

Lewis Kirshner   Associate Clinical Professor, Harvard Medical School; Affiliate Professor, Université Lyon 2, Département de Psychopathologie Clinique; Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute; faculty, Massachusetts Institute of Psychoanalysis.

Although psychoanalytic thought in France is known to American readers mainly through a small selection of the writings of André Green, Jacques Lacan, and Jean Laplanche, there are many important French authors who deserve to be read more widely. Among those I would include here, beside Piera Aulagnier, Laurence Kahn, Michel de M'Uzan, J.-B. Pontalis, and René Roussillion, is the scholarly analyst René Kaes, distinguished professor of psychology at the University of Lyon. Conversant with the German of Freud's original texts and widely read in the international psychoanalytic literature, Kaes incorporates a wide spectrum of ideas, ranging from the philosophically abstract to the complexities of clinical practice and the spadework of empirical research. His specialty has been the psychoanalytic theory and practice of therapeutic work with groups, a modality more widespread in France than in the U.S. Since his first publication, L'appareil psychique groupal: Constructions du groupe (1976), Kaes has developed a conception of the shared psychic space of the group, notably in relation to the broader issues of the complex question of “how the singular subject and the I (le Je), capable of assuming their place within an intersubjective ensemble, are constituted, transformed, or effaced” through the diverse modalities of membership in groups (Kaes 1993, p. 11; translation mine throughout).

Although his work returns repeatedly to the literature on group process (Foulkes, Bion, Anzieu, Neri, Pines) and to clinical experience with groups and families, the paradoxical nature of subjectivity as both a property of individuals and inseparable from membership in a group has been Kaes's constant preoccupation since his earliest papers. The argument is set forth in the important monograph Transmission de la

responseSet.title: The Dilemma of Contemporary Psychoanalysis: Toward a “Knowing” Post-Postmodernism
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Dilemma of Contemporary Psychoanalysis: Toward a “Knowing” Post-Postmodernism

Aner Govrin

Aner Govrin

28 Mapu Street
Tel Aviv 63434
Israel
E-mail: er22an@yahoo.com

Aner Govrin   Faculty, Department of Hermeneutics, Bar Ilan University; clinical psychologist, Tel Aviv Institute for Contemporary Psychoanalysis.

It is in the best interest of psychoanalysis that new schools of thought (earlier examples of which are the classical, Kleinian, and self psychological perspectives) evolve. Generating a new school of thought, however, is almost impossible in this postmodern era. The postmodern paradigm in psychoanalysis (intersubjectivity, dialectical constructivism, two-person psychology) does not strive to generate a “true” theory of mind that claims to fit reality, nor does it claim to be another school of thought. The creators of the classical, interpersonal, self psychological, ego psychological, Kleinian, Bionian, Fairbairnian, Winnicottian, and other schools cannot be postmodernists, for they all believed that their theories corresponded to reality and were therefore true. A shortage of analysts who “know the truth” today will make it extremely difficult for new schools of thought to arise in psychoanalysis, or for new and compelling theories and descriptions of the human psyche to be constructed. What is required is for pluralistic psychoanalytic institutions to empower analysts who are actively involved in the pursuit of new ideas and theories in psychoanalysis.

Friedman's Corpus Since His Anatomy: A Psychoanalytic Odyssey

Nathan Kravis

Nathan Kravis

141 East 55th Street, Suite 2F
New York, NY 10022
E-mail: nkravis@worldnet.att.net

Nathan Kravis   Clinical Associate Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College; Associate Director, Cornell History of Psychiatry Institute; Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

Lawrence Friedman is a preeminent theorist, critic, and commentator in American psychoanalysis today. The major themes of his work, beginning with his magnum opus, The Anatomy of Psychotherapy (1988), are examined in an effort to identify conceptual shifts and problems in contemporary psychoanalysis. In the years since publication of that work, Friedman's texts have increasingly focused on the epistemological controversies faced by contemporary psychoanalysts. He has become a vocal critic of recent developments in relational psychoanalysis, advancing views that are explored here and subjected to critical appraisal. To better understand Friedman's ideas, areas of disagreement with other authors, including Schafer, Renik, Hoffman, Mitchell, Rorty, and Michels, are explored. Finally, Friedman is presented as a crypto-intersubjectivist whose work effects a realignment of theory with practice.

The Absence of A Future: The Effect of Past Experience and Current Developmental Conflicts on a Midlife Analysis

Calvin Colarusso

Calvin Colarusso

1020 Prospect Street, Suite 415B
La Jolla, CA 92037
r.cogan@ttu.edu

Calvin Colarusso   Training and Supervising Analyst in Adult and Child Psychoanalysis, San Diego Psychoanalytic Institute and Society; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of California at San Diego School of Medicine.

The four-year analysis of Mr. T., a highly intelligent, successful professional who entered analysis primarily because of a depression is presented; he had had no previous therapeutic experience but was very curious about analysis. The analytic work led to the discovery of a deeply held unconscious conviction of premature death that was related to interaction among the effects of a traumatic parental divorce during childhood, estrangement from both parents prior to their deaths, and signs of aging and illness in midlife. Before this analytic work the patient was unable to conceptualize, let alone plan for, late-life developmental experiences such as retirement and grandparenthood. Transference-countertransference themes are discussed, as is the importance of an understanding of midlife developmental concepts to successful analytic work with patients in this age group.

Mirror Neurons and Intentional Attunement: Commentary on Olds

Vittorio Gallese

Vittorio Gallese

Parma
Italy
E-mail: vittorio.gallese@unipr.it
Web page: http://www.unipr.it/arpa/mirror/english/staff/gallese.htm

Vittorio Gallese   Associate Professor, Department of Neuroscience, University of Parma, Italy.

A path leads from identification by way of imitation to empathy, that is, to the comprehension of the mechanism by means of which we are enabled to take up any attitude at all towards another mental life.

—Sigmund Freud (1921, p. 110)

Psychoanalysis has always identified the body as the source of the energies alimenting psychic representations. Interestingly, recent developments in cognitive neuroscience have emphasized the role of the acting body and of sensorimotor systems in constituting the way our minds represent reality, by shaping our cognitive schemas (Gallese and Umiltà 2002; Gallese, Keysers, and Rizzolatti 2004; Gallese 2005a, b).

In his very timely and stimulating paper David Olds discusses the relevance of some recent neuroscientific discoveries for a rethinking of psychoanalytic theory and clinical practice. In particular, he emphasizes the possible role played by mirror neurons in building identity relations, implying that they could provide a subpersonal explanatory framework for intersubjectivity. Olds's hypothesis is important because it suggests a specific topic for a fruitful multidisciplinary exchange between psychoanalytic theory and clinical practice and cognitive neuroscience.

This work, as part of the European Science Foundation EUROCORES Programme OMLL, was supported by funds from the Italian C.N.R. and the EC Sixth Framework Programme under Contract no. ERAS-CT.

Talking about Medication

Robert Glick and Steven Roose

Steven Roose

125 East 84th Street
New York, NY 10028
